industry.name: Additive Manufacturing
industry.description: 3D printing, metal fabrication, and advanced materials — one of the fastest-growing sectors in U.S. defense, aerospace, and medical manufacturing. 84 roles across 5 clusters.

roles.title: AM Design Engineer 1
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Engineering or a related technical field is the standard educational pathway for this role. Common majors include Mechanical Engineering, Industrial Design, or Materials Science. While not strictly required, foundational knowledge in additive manufacturing principles or coursework in CAD software is highly advantageous. Pursuing certifications in specific CAD platforms or completing introductory courses in Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM) can further strengthen your application.

skills.name: CAD Software Proficiency
skills.description: Mastery of CAD tools like SolidWorks or Fusion 360 is essential for creating and modifying complex 3D models for additive manufacturing.

skills.name: DfAM Principles
skills.description: Understanding Design for Additive Manufacturing principles allows you to create parts optimized for 3D printing, maximizing benefits like lightweighting and consolidation.

skills.name: 3D Model Preparation
skills.description: Skilled in preparing digital 3D models for printing, including mesh repair, orientation, and support structure considerations.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing
skills.description: Applying GD&T knowledge ensures that manufactured parts meet precise dimensional and functional requirements, critical for performance.

skills.name: Technical Drawing Interpretation
skills.description: Ability to read and interpret technical drawings and specifications to accurately translate design intent into 3D models.

skills.name: Problem-Solving
skills.description: Aptitude for identifying and resolving design challenges encountered during the modeling and preparation phases for 3D printing.

roles.description: As an entry-level AM Design Engineer, you will be instrumental in transforming innovative product concepts into tangible realities through the power of additive manufacturing. Your typical day involves creating and refining intricate 3D digital models using CAD software, meticulously optimizing them for the unique requirements of 3D printing processes. You'll translate design specifications into detailed build files, ensuring manufacturability and performance. You are responsible for the accuracy and printability of the digital assets, collaborating closely with senior engineers and production teams to troubleshoot and iterate on designs. This role is perfect for a detail-oriented problem-solver with a passion for emerging technologies and a knack for spatial reasoning, eager to contribute to cutting-edge product development within the additive manufacturing value chain.

roles.title: DfAM Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Aerospace Engineering, Materials Science, or a related field is typically required for a DfAM Engineer. Strong coursework in CAD, FEA, and materials science is essential. While not always mandatory, relevant certifications in additive manufacturing processes or software like nTopology can be highly beneficial. Some roles may prefer candidates with a Master's degree or specialized postgraduate study in additive manufacturing technologies.

skills.name: Design For Additive Manufacturing
skills.description: You will apply principles to create parts optimized for 3D printing, considering process limitations and leveraging unique design freedoms.

skills.name: Topology Optimization
skills.description: You will use software to find the most efficient material distribution within a given design space, reducing weight and improving performance.

skills.name: Lattice Structures
skills.description: You will design and implement complex internal lattice geometries to achieve specific mechanical properties and significant lightweighting.

skills.name: Lightweighting Techniques
skills.description: You will focus on reducing component mass through design modifications while maintaining or improving structural integrity and functionality.

skills.name: Advanced CAD Software
skills.description: Proficiency in parametric and direct modeling tools is essential for creating and manipulating complex, optimized designs.

skills.name: FEA Simulation
skills.description: You will use Finite Element Analysis to predict component performance under load, validating design improvements and ensuring structural integrity.

skills.name: nTopology Software
skills.description: Expertise in this specialized software is highly valued for its advanced capabilities in generative design and complex geometry creation.

roles.description: As a DfAM Engineer in Additive Manufacturing, you will be at the forefront of innovation, specializing in redesigning components to leverage the unique capabilities of 3D printing. Your daily tasks involve utilizing advanced software to implement design freedoms like lattice structures, part consolidation, and topology optimization, aiming for significant weight reduction and enhanced performance. You're responsible for translating complex engineering challenges into manufacturable, optimized designs, often collaborating with manufacturing teams to ensure process feasibility. This role is crucial in the value chain, bridging design intent with production reality. A successful DfAM Engineer is analytical, creative, detail-oriented, and thrives on solving intricate design problems within the dynamic additive manufacturing landscape.

roles.title: AM Applications Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree is typically required for an AM Applications Engineer. Common majors include Mechanical Engineering, Materials Science, Manufacturing Engineering, or a related technical field. While not always mandatory, a Master's degree or relevant certifications in additive manufacturing processes or design can provide a competitive edge. Practical experience and a strong understanding of AM principles are often valued as highly as formal academic credentials.

skills.name: Customer Needs Analysis
skills.description: You translate client challenges and requirements into specific technical objectives for additive manufacturing solutions. This involves understanding their application and desired outcomes.

skills.name: AM Process Selection
skills.description: You evaluate and recommend the most suitable additive manufacturing technologies (e.g., FDM, SLA, SLS, DMLS) based on application requirements, material properties, and performance needs.

skills.name: Design for Additive Manufacturing
skills.description: You optimize part designs for AM processes, considering factors like printability, support structures, material behavior, and post-processing to ensure successful builds.

skills.name: Build Simulation & Analysis
skills.description: You utilize simulation tools to predict and mitigate potential build issues, such as warping or residual stress, ensuring part integrity and performance.

skills.name: Materials Science Understanding
skills.description: You possess knowledge of various AM materials, their properties, and how they perform in different applications and processes, guiding material selection.

skills.name: Cross-functional Communication
skills.description: You effectively communicate technical information to both engineering teams and non-technical stakeholders, ensuring alignment and project success.

roles.description: As an AM Applications Engineer, you are the crucial link between customer needs and the capabilities of additive manufacturing. Your day-to-day involves deep dives into potential AM use cases, meticulously evaluating their feasibility and selecting the optimal AM processes and materials. You'll be responsible for guiding applications from initial concept through design iteration, simulation, and rigorous testing to deliver a qualified, production-ready part. This role demands strong analytical skills, a passion for problem-solving, and the ability to translate complex technical requirements into actionable engineering plans. You thrive when you can bridge technical challenges with innovative manufacturing solutions, ensuring successful adoption of AM technologies for diverse customer projects.

roles.title: Senior AM Design Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Aerospace Engineering, Materials Science, or a related engineering field is typically required for this role. While a Bachelor's is the standard, a Master's degree or relevant professional certifications in Additive Manufacturing or specific design software can be advantageous. Some organizations may consider candidates with extensive, directly relevant experience in lieu of a formal degree, particularly if coupled with a strong portfolio showcasing advanced AM design capabilities.

skills.name: Advanced DfAM Principles
skills.description: Expertly applies Design for Additive Manufacturing principles to create optimized, lightweight, and high-performance parts specifically for AM processes.

skills.name: Simulation-Driven Design
skills.description: Leverages advanced simulation tools to predict part performance, validate designs, and iterate rapidly, ensuring optimal material usage and structural integrity.

skills.name: Multi-Process Expertise
skills.description: Possesses deep knowledge of diverse AM technologies (e.g., FDM, SLS, DMLS, SLA) and their respective design considerations and limitations.

skills.name: Design Standards Development
skills.description: Establishes and maintains robust design guidelines and best practices for AM part development within the organization.

skills.name: Technical Mentoring
skills.description: Guides and develops junior engineering talent, fostering their growth in AM design techniques and problem-solving skills.

skills.name: Material Science Understanding
skills.description: Applies knowledge of AM material properties and behavior to inform design decisions and ensure component reliability.

roles.description: As a Senior AM Design Engineer, you are a pivotal figure in driving innovation within the additive manufacturing (AM) sector. Your day-to-day involves leading intricate design programs for complex AM parts, ensuring they meet stringent performance and manufacturability requirements across various printing technologies. You'll be instrumental in establishing and refining design standards, acting as a technical authority and mentor to junior engineers, guiding them in advanced design principles. This role demands a proactive individual who thrives on tackling challenging engineering problems, possesses a deep understanding of AM processes, and excels at translating conceptual designs into tangible, high-value components. Your expertise is crucial in optimizing designs for AM, pushing the boundaries of what's possible in rapid prototyping and production.

roles.title: AM Simulation Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Graduate Degree is typically required for this role, with a Master's or Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering, Materials Science, or a related field being common. Focus on coursework in Finite Element Analysis (FEA), computational mechanics, and thermodynamics. While specific certifications aren't always mandatory, advanced study in simulation software relevant to additive manufacturing, such as Simufact or Additive Works, is highly beneficial. Practical research experience in simulation or additive manufacturing processes can also be a strong indicator of a candidate's preparedness.

skills.name: Build Simulation
skills.description: Develop and run simulations to predict the outcome of additive manufacturing builds, identifying potential failures before they occur.

skills.name: Thermal Distortion Modeling
skills.description: Analyze and predict how heat during the printing process will cause parts to warp or deform, optimizing designs to mitigate this.

skills.name: Residual Stress Analysis
skills.description: Quantify and understand the internal stresses that develop within a part during printing, which can impact its mechanical properties.

skills.name: Finite Element Analysis (FEA)
skills.description: Apply FEA principles to model complex physical phenomena, such as stress, strain, and heat transfer, within additive manufactured parts.

skills.name: Simufact/Additive Works Expertise
skills.description: Proficiently utilize specialized simulation software like Simufact or Additive Works to accurately model the AM process.

skills.name: Design Optimization
skills.description: Collaborate with design teams to modify part geometries and print strategies based on simulation feedback for improved manufacturability.

roles.description: As an AM Simulation Engineer, you are the critical bridge between digital design and physical additive manufacturing. Your day-to-day involves developing and executing sophisticated simulations to predict how parts will behave during the printing process. You’ll be responsible for analyzing potential issues like thermal distortion, residual stress, and build failures, using your expertise to optimize designs and print parameters before committing to production. This role is vital for minimizing material waste, reducing costly print failures, and ensuring the final part meets stringent performance requirements. You fit into the value chain by providing essential data-driven insights that de-risk the manufacturing process. A detail-oriented individual with strong analytical skills and a passion for problem-solving will thrive here, constantly seeking ways to improve the efficiency and reliability of additive manufacturing.

roles.title: Principal AM Engineer
roles.degree_detail: To excel as a Principal AM Engineer, a Graduate Degree is typically required, often a Master's or Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering, Materials Science, or a related engineering discipline. Advanced coursework in additive manufacturing processes, materials characterization, and design for additive manufacturing is highly beneficial. Relevant professional certifications or extensive post-graduate research focused on AM technologies may also be considered.

skills.name: AM Technology Strategy
skills.description: You will define the long-term vision and roadmap for additive manufacturing adoption, identifying emerging technologies and their strategic business impact.

skills.name: R&D Leadership
skills.description: You will lead and mentor research teams, guiding the development of new AM processes, materials, and applications from concept to validation.

skills.name: Qualification Frameworks
skills.description: You will establish and oversee rigorous qualification processes to ensure the reliability, performance, and regulatory compliance of AM components.

skills.name: IP Development
skills.description: You will identify opportunities for intellectual property creation and guide the patent filing process to protect novel AM innovations and designs.

skills.name: Advanced Materials Expertise
skills.description: You possess deep knowledge of polymers, metals, and ceramics for AM, understanding their properties, processing, and application suitability.

skills.name: Design for AM
skills.description: You will champion and guide the principles of designing parts optimized for additive manufacturing processes, leveraging unique capabilities.

roles.description: As a Principal AM Engineer, you are the visionary leader guiding additive manufacturing strategy and innovation within an organization. You will spearhead research and development initiatives, exploring novel materials, processes, and applications to push the boundaries of what's possible. Your responsibilities extend to defining and implementing robust qualification frameworks, ensuring the reliability and manufacturability of AM parts for critical applications. Furthermore, you'll be instrumental in developing and protecting intellectual property, safeguarding the company's competitive edge. This role is perfect for a strategic thinker with deep technical expertise, a passion for innovation, and the ability to inspire cross-functional teams towards ambitious goals. You thrive on solving complex challenges and shaping the future of manufacturing.

roles.title: AM Materials Lab Technician
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree is the typical educational foundation for this role. Common fields of study include materials science technology, mechanical engineering technology, or laboratory science. While not always mandatory, relevant certifications in laboratory safety or specific metrology techniques can be advantageous. Some individuals may pursue further specialized training or certificate programs in additive manufacturing processes to enhance their qualifications for this position.

skills.name: Powder Handling And Characterization
skills.description: Safely handle and prepare various feedstock powders for testing. Perform basic characterization tests to assess powder properties like particle size and flowability.

skills.name: Sample Preparation Techniques
skills.description: Prepare 3D printed parts and material samples for analysis. This includes sectioning, mounting, polishing, and etching to reveal microstructural details.

skills.name: Laboratory Safety Protocols
skills.description: Adhere strictly to all lab safety guidelines and procedures. Understand and implement safe handling practices for chemicals, equipment, and materials.

skills.name: Basic Metrology And Testing
skills.description: Utilize basic measurement tools and equipment to perform routine tests. This includes hardness, tensile testing, and dimensional verification.

skills.name: Data Recording And Documentation
skills.description: Accurately record experimental data and observations. Maintain detailed lab notebooks and reports for traceability and analysis by engineers.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Fundamentals
skills.description: Possess a foundational understanding of common AM processes. Recognize how material properties influence print outcomes and part performance.

roles.description: As an Entry-Level AM Materials Lab Technician, you are a crucial support for the Materials & Process Development team within the Additive Manufacturing industry. Your day-to-day involves meticulously preparing and characterizing feedstock powders and printed test specimens, ensuring the integrity of materials used in 3D printing. You'll assist materials engineers by conducting routine tests, maintaining laboratory equipment, and meticulously documenting results. This role is ideal for someone detail-oriented, safety-conscious, and eager to learn the intricacies of advanced materials. You thrive in a hands-on environment, contributing directly to the development and validation of new AM materials and processes, making you an essential part of the innovation pipeline.

roles.title: AM Materials Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Materials Science, Metallurgical Engineering, Chemical Engineering, or a related field is typically required. Relevant coursework should focus on material properties, processing, and characterization. While not always mandatory, pursuing advanced studies or certifications in additive manufacturing or specific material classes can be advantageous. Industry experience is often valued as highly as formal education.

skills.name: Powder Metallurgy
skills.description: Expertise in the science and technology of producing metal powders, including atomization and characterization, crucial for AM feedstock quality.

skills.name: Alloy Development
skills.description: Ability to design and modify metal alloys to achieve desired properties like strength, ductility, and corrosion resistance for 3D printing applications.

skills.name: Microstructure Analysis
skills.description: Proficiency in examining the internal structure of materials using microscopy to understand their behavior and predict performance.

skills.name: Laser-Material Interaction
skills.description: Understanding how laser energy affects material properties, including melting, solidification, and phase transformations during the printing process.

skills.name: Polymer Science
skills.description: Knowledge of polymer chemistry, processing, and characterization, essential for developing and qualifying polymer-based AM materials.

skills.name: Statistical Process Control
skills.description: Applying statistical methods to monitor and control material properties and printing processes, ensuring consistent quality and performance.

roles.description: As an AM Materials Engineer, you will be instrumental in developing and qualifying the metal and polymer feedstocks essential for additive manufacturing processes. Your day-to-day will involve meticulous study of how these materials respond to the intense energy of lasers and electron beams, ensuring consistent performance and quality. You'll be responsible for characterizing material properties, optimizing compositions, and validating new feedstocks for production. Collaborating closely with design and process engineers, you bridge the gap between material science and manufacturing, ensuring the printed parts meet stringent specifications. This role is perfect for a curious, analytical individual with a passion for material innovation and a knack for problem-solving in a fast-paced, cutting-edge environment.

roles.title: AM Process Development Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in a relevant engineering or science field is required for this role. Common majors include Materials Science and Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Manufacturing Engineering. While not always mandatory, a strong academic record with a focus on materials processing or advanced manufacturing is highly valued. Some individuals may pursue Master's degrees or relevant certifications in areas like statistical process control or specific additive manufacturing technologies to enhance their qualifications.

skills.name: Process Parameter Optimization
skills.description: Fine-tuning machine settings like laser power, scan speed, and hatch spacing to achieve optimal material properties and build quality for specific alloys and geometries.

skills.name: Design of Experiments (DOE)
skills.description: Systematically planning and executing experiments to efficiently identify key process variables and their interactions, leading to robust and repeatable build parameters.

skills.name: LPBF/DED Expertise
skills.description: In-depth knowledge and hands-on experience with Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) or Directed Energy Deposition (DED) processes, including equipment operation and troubleshooting.

skills.name: Statistical Process Control (SPC)
skills.description: Utilizing statistical methods to monitor and control the additive manufacturing process, ensuring consistency and identifying deviations before they impact part quality.

skills.name: Materials Characterization
skills.description: Understanding and applying techniques to evaluate material properties, such as tensile testing, microscopy (SEM/optical), and hardness, to assess build success.

skills.name: Failure Analysis
skills.description: Investigating the root causes of build failures or defects, employing analytical skills to implement corrective actions and improve process reliability.

roles.description: As an AM Process Development Engineer in Additive Manufacturing, you are instrumental in bringing novel materials and complex geometries to life through 3D printing. Your day-to-day involves meticulously calibrating print parameters, conducting Design of Experiments (DOE), and analyzing build outcomes to ensure density, minimize defects, and guarantee repeatability. You're responsible for validating new process parameters and troubleshooting issues that arise during development. You bridge the gap between material science and manufacturing, working closely with materials scientists and production teams to scale up successful processes. A successful engineer in this role is analytical, detail-oriented, and thrives on problem-solving in a fast-paced, innovative environment, possessing a deep understanding of metal additive manufacturing technologies.

roles.title: Senior AM Materials Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Graduate Degree is typically required for this senior role, with a Master's or Ph.D. in Materials Science and Engineering, Metallurgy, or a closely related field being common. Specialized coursework in areas like physical metallurgy, powder metallurgy, or materials characterization is highly beneficial. While formal certifications are less common, a strong academic foundation and demonstrated research capabilities are paramount. Experience in lieu of a specific degree is sometimes considered if accompanied by extensive, relevant professional achievements.

skills.name: Advanced Alloy R&D
skills.description: Spearhead the development of novel metal alloys specifically tailored for additive manufacturing processes, focusing on optimizing properties like strength, ductility, and thermal resistance.

skills.name: Materials Qualification
skills.description: Establish and execute rigorous testing protocols to qualify new and existing materials for critical aerospace and industrial applications, ensuring compliance with industry standards.

skills.name: Failure Analysis Expertise
skills.description: Investigate and resolve complex material failures in additively manufactured parts, identifying root causes and implementing corrective actions to prevent recurrence.

skills.name: AM Process Understanding
skills.description: Possess deep knowledge of various additive manufacturing techniques (e.g., DMLS, SLM, EBM) and their impact on material behavior and microstructure.

skills.name: Cross-Program Strategy
skills.description: Develop and manage a comprehensive materials roadmap that supports multiple concurrent engineering programs, anticipating future needs and technological trends.

skills.name: Data Analysis and Interpretation
skills.description: Analyze extensive material testing data to draw conclusions, validate hypotheses, and inform design and manufacturing decisions for AM components.

roles.description: As a Senior AM Materials Engineer in Additive Manufacturing, you will be at the forefront of innovation, leading critical materials research and development initiatives. Your day-to-day will involve deep dives into alloy development, rigorous qualification processes, and intricate failure analysis to ensure the integrity and performance of AM components across various programs. You'll own the strategic alloy roadmap, making crucial decisions that shape future material capabilities. This role requires a proactive individual who excels at problem-solving complex material challenges and can effectively communicate technical findings to cross-functional teams. You are responsible for the overall materials strategy, ensuring alignment with program objectives and driving advancements that push the boundaries of what's possible in additive manufacturing.

roles.title: AM Research Scientist
roles.degree_detail: A Graduate Degree is essential for this senior role, typically a Master's or, more commonly, a PhD in a relevant scientific or engineering field. Common majors include Materials Science, Chemical Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, or Physics, with a strong emphasis on materials or manufacturing processes. Advanced postdoctoral research experience is highly valued and often considered equivalent to some years of professional experience. While specific certifications are less common, a strong publication record in peer-reviewed journals is expected.

skills.name: Fundamental AM Research
skills.description: Conducting in-depth investigations into the core principles and emerging phenomena of additive manufacturing processes and materials.

skills.name: Novel Process Development
skills.description: Creating and validating new additive manufacturing techniques, optimizing parameters for improved performance, quality, and scalability.

skills.name: Materials Science Expertise
skills.description: Deep understanding of material properties, behavior, and development, specifically as they relate to AM applications and limitations.

skills.name: Experimental Design & Analysis
skills.description: Proficiency in designing rigorous experiments, collecting precise data, and employing advanced statistical methods for interpretation.

skills.name: Publication & Dissemination
skills.description: Communicating research findings effectively through high-impact publications, conference presentations, and technical reports.

skills.name: Grant Writing & Acquisition
skills.description: Securing research funding by developing compelling proposals and demonstrating the scientific and commercial merit of proposed projects.

roles.description: As a Senior AM Research Scientist in Materials & Process Development, you will spearhead groundbreaking research to advance additive manufacturing technologies. Your daily work involves designing and executing experiments, analyzing complex data, and developing novel materials and processes. You are responsible for pushing the boundaries of AM, often publishing findings and seeking grant funding to support your initiatives. This role bridges academic rigor with industrial application, requiring you to stay ahead of emerging trends. A successful scientist in this position is deeply curious, possesses strong analytical skills, and thrives in a collaborative environment where innovation is paramount. You will play a critical role in shaping the future of AM materials and processes.

roles.title: 3D Printer Operator
roles.degree_detail: A High School Diploma or GED is the standard educational requirement for this entry-level position. While formal degrees are not typically mandatory, vocational training or a certificate in manufacturing technology, machining, or a related field can be highly beneficial. Some employers may prefer candidates with coursework in computer-aided design (CAD) or familiarity with automated systems. On-the-job training is often provided, making a strong willingness to learn essential.

skills.name: Machine Setup And Calibration
skills.description: Proficiently prepares 3D printing equipment for operation by following manufacturer guidelines and job specifications. Ensures all parameters are correctly configured for successful builds.

skills.name: Material Handling And Loading
skills.description: Safely and accurately loads various additive manufacturing feedstocks, such as filaments, powders, or resins, into printers. Manages material inventory and storage.

skills.name: Print Job Monitoring
skills.description: Actively observes ongoing print jobs for anomalies, quality issues, or potential failures. Makes necessary adjustments to maintain print integrity and efficiency.

skills.name: Basic Machine Maintenance
skills.description: Performs routine cleaning, lubrication, and minor upkeep on 3D printers. Identifies and reports any mechanical issues to supervisors for prompt resolution.

skills.name: Quality Control Checks
skills.description: Conducts visual inspections of finished parts to ensure they meet dimensional accuracy and surface finish requirements. Documents inspection results.

skills.name: Troubleshooting Common Issues
skills.description: Identifies and addresses basic operational problems encountered during the printing process. Seeks assistance for more complex technical challenges.

roles.description: As an entry-level 3D Printer Operator in Additive Manufacturing, you are the hands-on professional responsible for bringing digital designs to life. Your day-to-day involves meticulously setting up and calibrating 3D printers, ensuring they are ready for production. You'll carefully load the correct feedstock materials, initiate the printing process, and diligently monitor build jobs to ensure quality and prevent failures. Performing routine maintenance, such as cleaning print chambers and checking components, is crucial for optimal machine performance. You are a vital link in the production chain, directly contributing to the creation of prototypes, tools, and end-use parts. Individuals who are detail-oriented, patient, and possess a strong mechanical aptitude will thrive in this foundational role.

roles.title: AM Machine Technician
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in a relevant technical field, such as Advanced Manufacturing Technology or Industrial Maintenance, is typically required for this entry-level position. Common majors include mechatronics, engineering technology, or a related discipline. While not always mandatory, relevant certifications in areas like Lean Manufacturing or specific machine operation can be advantageous. Some individuals may pursue further specialized training or apprenticeships to deepen their expertise in additive processes.

skills.name: LPBF/FDM Operation
skills.description: Proficiently operate Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) or Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) printers, understanding machine parameters and build chamber environments.

skills.name: Machine Calibration
skills.description: Perform precise calibrations of machine systems, including build plates, lasers, or extruders, to ensure dimensional accuracy and part quality.

skills.name: Preventive Maintenance
skills.description: Execute routine preventive maintenance tasks on additive manufacturing equipment to maximize uptime and prevent unexpected failures.

skills.name: Build Troubleshooting
skills.description: Identify, diagnose, and resolve common build issues, such as adhesion problems or print defects, to minimize scrap and ensure successful prints.

skills.name: Inert Gas Handling
skills.description: Safely handle and manage inert gas systems (e.g., Argon, Nitrogen) used in LPBF processes, monitoring levels and ensuring proper flow.

skills.name: Quality Inspection Basics
skills.description: Conduct basic visual inspections of finished parts to identify any surface imperfections or deviations from specifications.

roles.description: As an entry-level AM Machine Technician in Additive Manufacturing, you will be the hands-on operator responsible for keeping our industrial 3D printing machines running smoothly. Your day-to-day will involve meticulously operating and monitoring machines, performing essential calibrations to ensure print accuracy, and diligently troubleshooting any build failures that arise. You'll be a critical link in the production chain, ensuring that parts are manufactured to specification and on schedule, often working across different shifts to meet demand. This role requires a detail-oriented individual who is eager to learn, possesses strong problem-solving abilities, and can work effectively both independently and as part of a production team. Your contributions are vital to delivering high-quality additive components.

roles.title: Senior AM Technician
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree is typically the minimum educational requirement for a Senior AM Technician. Common majors include Manufacturing Technology, Mechanical Engineering Technology, or specific Additive Manufacturing programs. While not always mandatory, relevant certifications in specific AM technologies or advanced coursework in materials science can be highly beneficial. Some employers may consider extensive, directly relevant experience in lieu of a formal degree.

skills.name: Advanced Troubleshooting
skills.description: Diagnose and resolve complex issues across multiple AM platforms and processes. Identify root causes of failures and implement effective corrective actions.

skills.name: Multi-Machine Operation
skills.description: Proficiently operate and monitor a variety of additive manufacturing machines simultaneously. Ensure optimal performance and adherence to build specifications.

skills.name: Process Monitoring
skills.description: Continuously observe and analyze critical process parameters during builds. Make real-time adjustments to maintain quality and prevent defects.

skills.name: Operator Training
skills.description: Develop and deliver training programs for junior technicians. Share best practices for machine operation, safety, and quality control.

skills.name: Quality Control
skills.description: Implement and enforce rigorous quality checks throughout the production cycle. Ensure final parts meet all design and performance requirements.

skills.name: Material Handling
skills.description: Safely and accurately handle various AM powders, resins, and support materials. Understand material properties and their impact on builds.

roles.description: As a Senior Additive Manufacturing Technician, you are the backbone of complex production in the AM facility. You'll spend your days overseeing intricate multi-machine operations, ensuring the highest quality output for critical components. Your responsibilities extend beyond operation; you'll be the go-to for advanced troubleshooting when issues arise, diving deep into process parameters and material science to find solutions. You also play a vital role in team development, training and mentoring newer operators, sharing your expertise to elevate the entire production floor. This role is perfect for a detail-oriented problem-solver who thrives in a dynamic, technically challenging environment and enjoys both hands-on work and knowledge transfer within the additive manufacturing value chain.

roles.title: AM Manufacturing Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in a relevant engineering field is typically required for this role. Common majors include Mechanical Engineering, Manufacturing Engineering, or Materials Science. While not always mandatory, relevant certifications in additive manufacturing processes or project management can be beneficial. Advanced study or specialized coursework in 3D printing technologies is also a plus.

skills.name: Production Workflow Management
skills.description: Oversee the end-to-end additive manufacturing production process, ensuring smooth transitions from design to finished part.

skills.name: Build Preparation
skills.description: Prepare and optimize CAD files and build parameters for successful and efficient 3D printing operations.

skills.name: Throughput Optimization
skills.description: Analyze and improve production bottlenecks to increase output and reduce lead times for additive manufacturing.

skills.name: Machine Scheduling
skills.description: Develop and manage efficient schedules for the 3D printer fleet to maximize utilization and meet production demands.

skills.name: Fleet Management
skills.description: Monitor and maintain the health and performance of multiple additive manufacturing machines, ensuring operational readiness.

skills.name: Process Troubleshooting
skills.description: Identify and resolve issues within the additive manufacturing workflow, from machine malfunctions to print failures.

roles.description: As an AM Manufacturing Engineer, you are the linchpin in scaling additive manufacturing from concept to consistent production. You'll meticulously plan and schedule build jobs, ensuring optimal machine utilization and maximizing throughput for serial production runs. Your responsibility extends to overseeing the entire production workflow, from preparing build files to managing the fleet of 3D printers and troubleshooting any operational issues that arise. You are crucial in bridging the gap between design iteration and reliable output, ensuring quality and efficiency. This role thrives on problem-solving, a keen eye for detail, and the ability to manage complex production schedules in a fast-paced environment.

roles.title: AM Production Supervisor
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's degree in a relevant technical field such as Manufacturing Engineering, Industrial Technology, or Materials Science is often preferred for an AM Production Supervisor. However, this requirement can sometimes be substituted with significant, directly applicable experience in additive manufacturing operations. Industry-specific certifications in lean manufacturing or Six Sigma can also strengthen an application. Some individuals may advance into this role from experienced operator positions with demonstrated leadership potential and further specialized training.

skills.name: Team Leadership and Development
skills.description: Inspire and guide your print floor team, fostering a collaborative environment and developing operator skills to maximize performance and efficiency.

skills.name: Production Scheduling and Planning
skills.description: Develop and manage detailed production schedules, ensuring optimal resource allocation and timely completion of additive manufacturing jobs.

skills.name: Safety Compliance and Enforcement
skills.description: Champion a strong safety culture, ensuring all team members adhere to protocols and regulations within the additive manufacturing facility.

skills.name: Quality Control and Assurance
skills.description: Oversee print quality and process adherence, implementing checks to ensure parts meet specifications and customer requirements.

skills.name: Process Optimization
skills.description: Analyze production data and workflows to identify bottlenecks and implement improvements for enhanced throughput and reduced waste.

skills.name: Material Handling and Management
skills.description: Ensure proper storage, handling, and tracking of diverse additive manufacturing materials to maintain integrity and availability.

roles.description: As an AM Production Supervisor in Additive Manufacturing, you are the linchpin of the print floor, ensuring that daily production goals are met with efficiency and precision. You'll lead and mentor your team of operators, overseeing their work, managing intricate production schedules, and upholding rigorous safety standards to maintain a secure and productive environment. Your primary responsibility is the successful execution of the production plan, closely monitoring key performance indicators to identify areas for improvement and drive continuous enhancement. This role requires a proactive, detail-oriented individual who excels at problem-solving and thrives in a fast-paced, technologically evolving setting, acting as a crucial link between strategic production planning and hands-on operational execution.

roles.title: AM Operations Manager
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree is typically required for this senior management role. Common and preferred majors include Manufacturing Engineering, Mechanical Engineering, Industrial Engineering, or Operations Management. While not always mandatory, a Master's degree in a related field or a PMP certification can be advantageous. Some organizations may consider extensive, directly relevant experience in lieu of a specific degree.

skills.name: Operations Strategy
skills.description: Develop and implement long-term strategies for additive manufacturing operations, aligning with company goals and market demands to ensure scalable production.

skills.name: Capacity Planning
skills.description: Forecast production needs, manage machine utilization, and optimize resource allocation to meet demand efficiently and cost-effectively across the AM fleet.

skills.name: Lean Manufacturing
skills.description: Apply lean principles to streamline production processes, eliminate waste, and enhance overall operational efficiency within the additive manufacturing environment.

skills.name: P&L Management
skills.description: Oversee the financial performance of the production unit, managing budgets, controlling costs, and driving profitability for additive manufacturing operations.

skills.name: Quality Assurance
skills.description: Establish and maintain rigorous quality control standards throughout the additive manufacturing process, ensuring product consistency and customer satisfaction.

skills.name: Supply Chain Coordination
skills.description: Liaise with suppliers and internal teams to ensure a smooth flow of materials and manage inventory for additive manufacturing production.

roles.description: As a Senior AM Operations Manager, you are the linchpin of our additive manufacturing production. You'll oversee the entire operational lifecycle, from strategic capacity planning and resource allocation to ensuring stringent quality control and on-time delivery across our diverse machine fleet. Your day-to-day involves optimizing workflows, managing P&L for the production unit, and implementing lean manufacturing principles to drive efficiency and cost-effectiveness. You are responsible for the performance and output of the entire production team and the machines they operate. This senior role requires a proactive leader with a deep understanding of AM processes, excellent problem-solving abilities, and a commitment to continuous improvement. A person who thrives in this role is analytical, decisive, and possesses strong leadership and communication skills.

roles.title: AM Post-Processing Technician
roles.degree_detail: A High School Diploma or equivalent is the minimum educational requirement for an entry-level AM Post-Processing Technician. While no specific degree is mandated, vocational training or coursework in manufacturing technology, materials science, or machining can be highly beneficial. Some employers may prefer candidates with certifications in specific finishing techniques or quality control, though these are often gained on the job. Continued learning through on-the-job training is common and encouraged.

skills.name: Support Removal
skills.description: Proficiently and safely detaching intricate support structures from 3D printed parts using specialized tools, ensuring no damage to the final component.

skills.name: Surface Finishing
skills.description: Applying techniques such as sanding, polishing, or media blasting to achieve desired surface roughness, aesthetics, and dimensional accuracy.

skills.name: Part Cleaning
skills.description: Thoroughly cleaning parts to remove uncured resins, powders, or debris using appropriate solvents and methods, preparing them for subsequent processes.

skills.name: Manual Dexterity
skills.description: Possessing fine motor skills and hand-eye coordination to manipulate small parts and tools precisely, essential for detailed post-processing tasks.

skills.name: Quality Inspection Basics
skills.description: Understanding fundamental visual inspection techniques to identify defects and ensure parts meet basic dimensional and aesthetic requirements.

skills.name: Tool Operation
skills.description: Safely operating various hand and power tools commonly used in post-processing, such as deburring tools, files, and cleaning equipment.

roles.description: As an entry-level AM Post-Processing Technician in Additive Manufacturing, you are crucial to transforming raw 3D printed parts into finished, functional components. Your day-to-day involves meticulous work like carefully removing support structures, cleaning excess material, and applying various finishing techniques to meet precise specifications. You'll be responsible for ensuring each part meets quality standards before it moves to the next stage of the value chain, whether that's inspection, assembly, or direct customer use. This role requires a keen eye for detail, patience, and a steady hand, making it ideal for individuals who enjoy hands-on work and take pride in producing high-quality results. Your contribution directly impacts the final usability and aesthetic of critical additively manufactured parts.

roles.title: AM Quality Technician
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree is the typical educational foundation for an AM Quality Technician. Common and relevant programs include Associate of Applied Science (AAS) degrees in Quality Assurance, Manufacturing Technology, or Mechanical Engineering Technology. While a Bachelor's degree is not usually required at this entry-level, foundational knowledge in metrology and quality systems is essential. Pursuing certifications like Certified Quality Technician (CQT) from ASQ can be beneficial.

skills.name: Dimensional Inspection
skills.description: Proficiency in verifying part dimensions against specifications using various measurement tools and techniques to ensure conformity.

skills.name: Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)
skills.description: Ability to operate CMM equipment for precise, automated measurement of complex part geometries and features.

skills.name: Calipers and Micrometers
skills.description: Skilled in using manual precision measuring instruments for accurate, hands-on dimensional checks of printed parts.

skills.name: Quality Documentation
skills.description: Competence in meticulously recording inspection results, non-conformance reports, and other quality data accurately.

skills.name: ISO Standards Familiarity
skills.description: Understanding of relevant ISO quality management system standards (e.g., ISO 9001) as they apply to manufacturing processes.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Processes
skills.description: Basic knowledge of common AM technologies (e.g., FDM, SLA, SLS) and their impact on part quality and inspection.

roles.description: As an entry-level AM Quality Technician in Additive Manufacturing, you are a crucial part of ensuring that 3D printed parts meet rigorous standards. Your day-to-day involves meticulously inspecting finished components using a variety of calibrated measurement tools, comparing them against engineering drawings and specifications. You'll be responsible for accurately documenting all quality control findings, identifying any deviations or defects. This role is fundamental to the post-processing workflow, directly impacting the reliability and performance of final products. You'll work closely with production teams to provide feedback for process improvement. An ideal candidate is detail-oriented, possesses strong analytical skills, and has a keen eye for precision, thriving in an environment where accuracy and adherence to quality protocols are paramount.

roles.title: NDT Technician (AM)
roles.degree_detail: To excel as an NDT Technician in Additive Manufacturing, an Associate's Degree is typically required, often in fields like Non-Destructive Testing, Mechanical Engineering Technology, or a related technical discipline. Many technicians also pursue specialized certifications, such as those offered by the American Society for Nondestructive Testing (ASNT), to validate their expertise. Continuous learning and advanced training in specific NDT methods relevant to AM are highly encouraged for career progression.

skills.name: CT Scanning
skills.description: Proficiency in operating industrial CT scanners to generate detailed 3D volumetric data of printed parts, revealing internal structures and defects.

skills.name: X-Ray Inspection
skills.description: Skilled in utilizing X-ray technology to detect internal discontinuities, material density variations, and foreign inclusions within AM components.

skills.name: Ultrasonic Testing
skills.description: Expertise in applying ultrasonic waves to identify internal flaws, measure wall thickness, and assess material integrity in printed parts.

skills.name: Defect Detection
skills.description: Ability to accurately identify and categorize various types of defects common in additive manufacturing, such as porosity, lack of fusion, and cracks.

skills.name: Porosity Analysis
skills.description: Competence in analyzing scan data to quantify and characterize internal porosity, a critical quality parameter for AM parts.

skills.name: Quality Assurance
skills.description: Understanding of quality control principles and documentation procedures to ensure parts meet specified standards and requirements.

roles.description: As an NDT Technician in Additive Manufacturing, you are the guardian of part integrity, meticulously inspecting 3D printed components before they reach customers or further stages of production. Your typical day involves operating advanced non-destructive testing equipment, such as CT scanners, X-ray machines, and ultrasonic devices, to identify internal flaws like voids, porosity, and micro-cracks. You are responsible for interpreting complex scan data, documenting findings accurately, and collaborating with engineers and production teams to troubleshoot issues and ensure parts meet stringent quality standards. This role is crucial in the post-processing and quality cluster, directly impacting product reliability and customer satisfaction. You thrive in this role if you possess a keen eye for detail, a methodical approach to problem-solving, and an aptitude for understanding complex technical data.

roles.title: AM Quality Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical, Manufacturing, or Materials Science Engineering is typically required to build the necessary technical foundation for this role. While a four-year degree is the standard entry point, candidates with a background in Industrial Engineering or Quality Systems Management are also highly competitive. Pursuing professional certifications such as a Six Sigma Green Belt or ASQ Certified Quality Engineer (CQE) can significantly enhance your candidacy. Advanced study, such as a Master’s degree in Additive Manufacturing or Systems Engineering, is often preferred for those looking to accelerate their career progression within highly regulated sectors.

skills.name: Quality Management Systems Compliance
skills.description: You must navigate and maintain complex quality frameworks like AS9100 or ISO 9001. This involves documenting processes and ensuring that all additive production steps strictly adhere to established regulatory requirements.

skills.name: Additive Part Qualification Protocols
skills.description: This involves validating that specific AM processes consistently produce parts meeting design specifications. You will oversee testing procedures, material characterization, and statistical validation to ensure long-term process stability.

skills.name: Advanced Metrology And Inspection
skills.description: You will utilize sophisticated measurement tools like 3D laser scanners, CT scanning, and coordinate measuring machines. Interpreting this data is vital for verifying geometric tolerances and internal porosity of complex lattice structures.

skills.name: Root Cause Analysis Methodologies
skills.description: When builds fail or parts deviate from specs, you lead the investigation using tools like Fishbone diagrams or 5 Whys. Your goal is to identify systemic issues and implement effective corrective actions.

skills.name: Statistical Process Control Implementation
skills.description: You apply statistical methods to monitor and control additive manufacturing processes. By analyzing data trends, you proactively identify potential quality drifts before they result in non-conforming parts or scrap.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: You are responsible for generating clear, precise technical reports and quality documentation. This ensures full traceability of parts from raw powder input to the final finished component for customer audits.

roles.description: As an Additive Manufacturing Quality Engineer, you serve as the critical bridge between design intent and physical part integrity within the production lifecycle. You are responsible for maintaining rigorous quality management systems, overseeing part qualification protocols, and executing root-cause investigations when deviations occur. Your day-to-day involves developing robust inspection plans, managing non-conformance reports, and ensuring strict compliance with industry standards like AS9100. You collaborate closely with production teams and design engineers to optimize build parameters and post-processing workflows, ensuring every component meets stringent aerospace or medical requirements. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, a deep curiosity for material science, and the ability to translate complex metrology data into actionable process improvements that drive operational excellence.

roles.title: Senior AM Quality Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Mechanical, Materials, or Manufacturing Engineering is the standard requirement for this senior-level position. While a four-year degree is essential, many employers highly value candidates who possess advanced certifications such as an ASQ Certified Quality Engineer or Six Sigma Black Belt. Additional graduate-level coursework in additive manufacturing or quality systems management is often preferred to navigate the complex regulatory landscapes of the industry. While the core engineering degree is mandatory, equivalent professional experience in high-stakes quality environments may occasionally supplement formal advanced education requirements.

skills.name: Regulatory Compliance And Qualification Frameworks
skills.description: You must navigate complex standards like AS9100 or ISO 13485 to ensure additive parts meet safety and reliability requirements. This involves developing validation strategies that satisfy stringent aerospace and medical regulatory bodies.

skills.name: Statistical Process Control And Analysis
skills.description: You will utilize advanced statistical methodologies to monitor build stability and powder quality. This skill is critical for identifying process drift and ensuring that every printed component maintains consistent material properties.

skills.name: Audit Leadership And Strategy
skills.description: You are responsible for owning the audit lifecycle, from preparing technical documentation to hosting external regulatory inspectors. You must represent the organization with confidence while maintaining a culture of constant compliance.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Post-Processing Validation
skills.description: You must define and validate the critical post-processing steps such as heat treatment, hot isostatic pressing, and surface finishing. Ensuring these secondary operations do not compromise the part's integrity is vital.

skills.name: Root Cause Analysis And Corrective Action
skills.description: You lead systematic investigations into build failures or material inconsistencies to prevent recurrence. This requires a deep understanding of the intersection between machine parameters and metallurgical outcomes in additive parts.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Quality Stakeholder Management
skills.description: You act as the quality champion, aligning design engineers, production technicians, and external clients on quality expectations. Effective communication is essential to integrate quality checkpoints seamlessly into the additive production workflow.

roles.description: As a Senior AM Quality Engineer, you serve as the primary authority for ensuring additive components meet the rigorous standards of highly regulated sectors like aerospace and medical device manufacturing. You lead the end-to-end qualification strategy, translating complex regulatory requirements into actionable process controls and robust validation protocols. Your day-to-day involves spearheading internal and external audits, managing non-conformance investigations, and driving continuous improvement initiatives across the production floor. You act as the bridge between design engineering and manufacturing, ensuring that quality is baked into the build parameters rather than inspected in. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, a deep passion for precision engineering, and the leadership presence required to influence cross-functional teams toward uncompromising quality excellence.

roles.title: AM Sales Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s degree is required for this role, typically in Mechanical Engineering, Manufacturing Engineering, or Industrial Design. While these technical backgrounds provide the necessary foundation for understanding print parameters and material properties, degrees in Business Administration with a strong technical minor are also considered. Pursuing professional certifications in specific AM technologies or project management methodologies like PMP can significantly enhance your candidacy. While formal education is a baseline, candidates who demonstrate a commitment to continuous learning through industry-specific workshops or additive-focused coursework often stand out in this competitive field.

skills.name: Technical Solution Selling
skills.description: This involves identifying client manufacturing bottlenecks and mapping them to specific additive hardware and material capabilities. You must articulate the value proposition of 3D printing over traditional methods to technical stakeholders.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Technology Proficiency
skills.description: You need a comprehensive understanding of various print processes such as PBF, DED, or Binder Jetting. This knowledge allows you to accurately advise clients on the best technology for their specific geometry and material requirements.

skills.name: Customer Demonstration And Presentation
skills.description: You are responsible for delivering persuasive technical demos that showcase machine performance and software workflows. This requires the ability to handle live troubleshooting while maintaining a professional and engaging narrative for potential buyers.

skills.name: Technical Proposal Development
skills.description: This skill focuses on writing detailed technical scopes of work, cost-benefit analyses, and print feasibility studies. Your proposals must clearly communicate complex technical data in a format that business leaders can easily interpret.

skills.name: CRM And Pipeline Management
skills.description: You must utilize CRM software to track lead progression, manage follow-ups, and forecast sales revenue accurately. Maintaining clean data is essential for managing a mid-level sales cycle and meeting quarterly performance quotas.

skills.name: Application Engineering Collaboration
skills.description: You work alongside internal application teams to validate printability and post-processing requirements for complex parts. This collaboration ensures that the solutions promised during the sales process are successfully delivered to the customer.

roles.description: As an Additive Manufacturing Sales Engineer, you serve as the critical technical bridge between complex 3D printing capabilities and customer manufacturing requirements. You spend your days conducting discovery sessions to identify pain points, performing high-impact hardware and software demonstrations, and crafting detailed technical proposals that justify the ROI of additive adoption. You are responsible for qualifying leads, managing the sales pipeline, and collaborating closely with application engineers to ensure proposed solutions are technically viable. You thrive in this role if you possess a blend of consultative sales acumen and deep curiosity regarding material science and machine kinematics. By translating engineering challenges into business value, you play a pivotal role in accelerating the industry-wide transition from traditional subtractive methods to advanced digital manufacturing workflows.

roles.title: AM Software Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Computer Science, Software Engineering, or Mechanical Engineering is the standard requirement for this position. While a four-year degree provides the necessary foundation in data structures and algorithmic design, candidates with a background in Computational Geometry or Robotics are highly preferred. While not strictly mandatory, pursuing a Master’s degree in Computer Graphics or Additive Manufacturing can significantly enhance your technical versatility. Professional certifications in specific CAD software ecosystems or cloud-based manufacturing platforms can also serve as valuable differentiators in this competitive field.

skills.name: Advanced Slicing Algorithm Development
skills.description: You will design and implement complex algorithms that convert 3D geometry into discrete, printable layers. This requires deep mathematical proficiency to optimize for print time, material usage, and structural integrity.

skills.name: C++ And Python Development
skills.description: You must utilize C++ for high-performance, low-latency computational tasks such as geometric processing, while using Python for rapid prototyping, scripting, and integrating various software modules within the AM pipeline.

skills.name: Toolpath Generation And Optimization
skills.description: This involves creating intelligent paths for print heads or lasers to follow. You will optimize these paths to minimize travel time and prevent thermal accumulation, ensuring high-quality part production.

skills.name: CAD API Integration
skills.description: You will work with industry-standard CAD kernels to extract and manipulate geometry data. Proficiency here allows you to build custom plugins and bridge the gap between design software and machine control.

skills.name: Build Prep Software Engineering
skills.description: You are responsible for the user-facing tools that allow operators to orient parts, generate support structures, and manage build trays. This requires balancing intuitive UI design with complex backend calculations.

skills.name: Computational Geometry Proficiency
skills.description: A solid grasp of computational geometry is essential for handling mesh manipulation, voxelization, and intersection detection. This skill ensures your software can accurately process even the most complex industrial geometries.

roles.description: As an AM Software Engineer, you bridge the critical gap between digital design and physical output by developing the sophisticated software ecosystem that drives additive manufacturing systems. You will spend your days architecting robust slicing algorithms, optimizing complex toolpath generation strategies, and integrating CAD APIs to ensure seamless data translation from 3D models to machine-ready instructions. Collaborating closely with mechanical engineers and material scientists, you are responsible for the reliability and efficiency of build-prep workflows that dictate print success. You thrive in this role if you possess a rigorous analytical mindset, a passion for high-performance computing, and the ability to solve geometric challenges that push the boundaries of current 3D printing technology while maintaining stringent industrial standards.

roles.title: AM Supply Chain Analyst
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree is the standard requirement for this entry-level position to ensure a foundational understanding of logistics and production principles. Common majors include Supply Chain Management, Industrial Engineering, Business Administration, or Operations Management. While not strictly mandatory, earning a certification like the APICS Certified Supply Chain Professional (CSCP) or pursuing coursework in additive manufacturing technologies can significantly enhance your candidacy. Many employers value a blend of technical coursework and practical internship experience over advanced degrees at this stage of your career.

skills.name: Inventory Management and Control
skills.description: You will oversee the lifecycle of specialized AM powders, ensuring optimal stock levels are maintained to support production schedules. This involves tracking consumption rates and preventing material degradation or contamination.

skills.name: ERP System Data Management
skills.description: Proficiency in enterprise resource planning software is essential for tracking procurement cycles and production orders. You will use these systems to generate accurate reports and maintain visibility across the supply chain.

skills.name: Feedstock Sourcing and Procurement
skills.description: You will coordinate with specialized vendors to procure high-quality metal or polymer materials that meet stringent industry standards. This requires evaluating supplier lead times and maintaining consistent material availability.

skills.name: Demand Planning and Forecasting
skills.description: By analyzing production throughput and historical machine usage, you will forecast future material requirements. This proactive approach ensures that the facility has the necessary resources to meet upcoming client project demands.

skills.name: Logistics and Vendor Coordination
skills.description: You are responsible for managing the inbound flow of materials, ensuring that shipping logistics align with production timelines. Strong communication is key to troubleshooting delays and maintaining reliable vendor partnerships.

skills.name: Data Analysis and Reporting
skills.description: You will leverage analytical tools to monitor supply chain performance metrics and identify potential bottlenecks. This data-driven approach helps the team optimize costs and improve overall production efficiency.

roles.description: As an Additive Manufacturing Supply Chain Analyst, you serve as the critical link between raw material procurement and high-precision production output. Your day-to-day involves monitoring complex feedstock inventory levels, managing vendor relationships for specialized metal or polymer powders, and executing demand planning to ensure machines never sit idle. You are responsible for maintaining data integrity within ERP systems, tracking lead times, and mitigating supply chain disruptions that could halt critical builds. You fit into the production team by ensuring the right materials arrive at the right time, balancing cost-efficiency with quality compliance. You will thrive in this role if you are detail-oriented, possess a analytical mindset, and can remain adaptable while navigating the fast-paced, evolving logistics of the 3D printing industry.

roles.title: Business Development Manager (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's degree is the standard requirement for this role, typically in Mechanical Engineering, Materials Science, or Business Administration. Candidates often benefit from a hybrid academic background that combines technical knowledge with a focus on marketing or supply chain management. While a Master’s degree is not strictly mandatory, an MBA is highly preferred for senior positions to demonstrate advanced commercial acumen. Professional certifications in project management or specialized additive manufacturing courses can further strengthen your candidacy by validating your expertise in industrial production standards.

skills.name: Strategic Additive Market Development
skills.description: You analyze industrial trends to identify high-growth sectors where additive manufacturing offers a distinct competitive advantage. This involves mapping complex supply chains to position 3D printing as a viable alternative to traditional subtractive manufacturing.

skills.name: Technical Value Proposition Articulation
skills.description: You translate complex technical specifications into compelling business cases for stakeholders. You must effectively communicate the ROI of additive processes, such as part consolidation, weight reduction, and faster time-to-market cycles, to non-technical executive leadership.

skills.name: Strategic Account Lifecycle Management
skills.description: You own the relationship with key enterprise accounts, guiding them from initial prototyping phases through to full-scale serial production. This requires managing expectations, solving integration challenges, and ensuring long-term client retention and revenue expansion.

skills.name: Complex Partnership Ecosystem Orchestration
skills.description: You cultivate and leverage a network of material suppliers, software providers, and post-processing partners to deliver end-to-end solutions. Building these alliances is critical for providing comprehensive service offerings that meet diverse manufacturing requirements.

skills.name: Advanced Pipeline And Revenue Forecasting
skills.description: You utilize data-driven methods to manage your sales funnel and provide accurate revenue projections to internal stakeholders. This skill ensures that production capacity is aligned with incoming demand and long-term business growth objectives.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Technical Collaboration
skills.description: You work closely with application engineers and production managers to ensure that promised capabilities align with operational reality. This collaboration is essential for maintaining quality standards and managing the technical feasibility of complex client projects.

roles.description: As a senior Business Development Manager in additive manufacturing, you spearhead revenue growth by identifying high-potential market segments and translating complex 3D printing capabilities into tangible business value for enterprise clients. You are responsible for architecting long-term sales strategies, nurturing strategic partnerships, and managing high-value accounts that require deep technical integration. Operating at the intersection of engineering and commercial operations, you act as the primary bridge between client requirements and internal production capabilities. You thrive in this role if you are a self-driven strategic thinker who possesses the technical fluency to articulate the benefits of additive workflows while maintaining the persistence required to navigate complex procurement cycles and influence stakeholders in a rapidly evolving industrial landscape.

roles.title: Director of Additive Manufacturing
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Industrial Engineering, or Materials Science is typically required to provide the foundational technical literacy necessary for this role. While a bachelor's degree is the standard entry point, many leaders in this space possess an MBA or a Master’s in Manufacturing Systems to bolster their strategic and financial decision-making capabilities. Professional certifications in Six Sigma Black Belt or PMP are highly preferred, as they demonstrate the process rigor required to manage large-scale AM deployments. While academic credentials are vital, they are often complemented by extensive industry experience that validates your ability to manage complex technical lifecycles.

skills.name: Strategic Additive Manufacturing Leadership
skills.description: You define the long-term vision for AM adoption, aligning technology investments with business objectives. This involves evaluating build-versus-buy scenarios and determining the optimal path for scaling production capabilities.

skills.name: Full P&L Management Accountability
skills.description: You maintain total ownership of the additive manufacturing budget, including capital expenditure for hardware and operational costs. You are responsible for driving profitability by optimizing machine utilization and material efficiency.

skills.name: Executive Stakeholder Communication
skills.description: You translate complex technical performance metrics into actionable business insights for the C-suite and board members. This skill is critical for securing funding and maintaining alignment on strategic manufacturing initiatives.

skills.name: Technology Roadmap Development
skills.description: You oversee the evaluation and integration of new AM platforms, materials, and post-processing technologies. Keeping the organization at the forefront of industry advancements is essential for sustaining long-term competitive advantage.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Organizational Leadership
skills.description: You lead diverse teams of engineers, designers, and supply chain specialists to ensure seamless project execution. Fostering collaboration across departments is vital for integrating additive workflows into the broader product lifecycle.

skills.name: Supply Chain Integration Strategy
skills.description: You manage the complexities of the AM supply chain, from raw material sourcing to final quality assurance. You ensure that additive processes are effectively integrated into traditional manufacturing and logistics environments.

roles.description: As a Director of Additive Manufacturing, you serve as the ultimate architect of your organization’s 3D printing ecosystem, bridging the gap between cutting-edge technical capabilities and commercial viability. You own the end-to-end P&L, defining the overarching strategy while steering technology roadmaps to ensure your firm maintains a competitive edge in distributed manufacturing. Your day-to-day involves high-level executive communication, aligning cross-functional stakeholders, and scaling operational teams to meet rigorous production demands. You thrive by balancing long-term innovation with immediate bottom-line impact, navigating complex supply chain integration, and fostering a culture of technical excellence. Success in this role requires a visionary mindset, financial acumen, and the ability to translate intricate additive processes into clear, value-driven business outcomes for your organization.

roles.title: AM CAD/Reverse Engineering Specialist
roles.degree_detail: While a formal degree is sometimes required for this role, employers often prioritize hands-on technical proficiency over specific credentials. A bachelor’s degree in Mechanical Engineering, Industrial Design, or Manufacturing Technology is highly valued, though an associate degree combined with significant portfolio experience is frequently accepted. Certifications in industry-standard software like Geomagic or specialized additive manufacturing courses can significantly bolster your candidacy. The path is flexible, provided you can demonstrate a strong command of 3D modeling principles and a practical understanding of how digital geometry translates into physical printed parts.

skills.name: Advanced Reverse Engineering Workflows
skills.description: You will utilize specialized software to convert raw 3D scan data into parametric CAD models. This process involves extracting geometric features and surfacing complex organic shapes for downstream additive manufacturing.

skills.name: High Resolution 3D Scanning
skills.description: Operating structured light or laser scanning hardware is essential for capturing accurate digital twins. You must understand scan alignment, point cloud registration, and noise reduction to ensure high-fidelity data acquisition.

skills.name: Mesh Repair And Optimization
skills.description: You will identify and fix non-manifold geometry, holes, and self-intersections within STL or OBJ files. Ensuring mesh integrity is critical to preventing print failures and achieving high-quality surface finishes.

skills.name: Parametric CAD Reconstruction
skills.description: Translating scanned data into editable feature-based models allows for iterative design improvements. Proficiency in industry-standard CAD platforms is necessary to create robust, manufacturing-ready geometry from legacy physical components.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Design Principles
skills.description: Understanding the constraints and opportunities of various print technologies is vital for success. You will apply design-for-additive-manufacturing principles to ensure your reconstructed models are optimized for support structures and build orientation.

skills.name: Specialized Software Proficiency
