skills.description: Mastery of industry-specific tools such as Geomagic Design X or Control is a core requirement. These platforms enable the seamless integration of scan data into the broader engineering and production ecosystem.

roles.description: As an Additive Manufacturing CAD and Reverse Engineering Specialist, you serve as the critical bridge between physical components and digital production. Your day-to-day involves operating high-resolution 3D scanners, processing complex point clouds, and meticulously reconstructing clean, manifold CAD models from legacy parts. You are responsible for ensuring geometric accuracy, repairing non-watertight meshes, and optimizing designs for successful additive fabrication. As a vital member of the engineering team, you enable rapid prototyping and digital inventory solutions by transforming physical assets into printable data. You will thrive in this role if you possess a keen eye for geometric detail, a patient approach to complex software workflows, and a genuine passion for modernizing traditional manufacturing processes through advanced digital tools.

roles.title: AM Automation Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Mechatronics, Robotics, or Electrical Engineering is typically required to build the necessary technical foundation for this role. While a four-year degree is the standard entry point, candidates with significant field experience in industrial automation may be considered. Pursuing professional certifications in PLC programming, industrial robotics, or additive manufacturing technologies can further strengthen your candidacy. While not strictly mandatory, an advanced degree or specialized coursework in systems engineering often provides a competitive edge when managing complex, interconnected production environments.

skills.name: Industrial Robotics And Integration
skills.description: You will program and deploy robotic arms for automated build plate removal and part extraction. This skill is critical for ensuring consistent, safe, and repeatable material handling within the additive manufacturing cell.

skills.name: PLC And Factory Automation
skills.description: Mastery of Programmable Logic Controllers is essential for managing the hardware interfaces of 3D printers and auxiliary equipment. You will use these systems to synchronize complex sequences across the entire production line.

skills.name: Digital Thread And Connectivity
skills.description: You must maintain the digital continuity of part data from CAD through post-processing. Implementing robust communication protocols ensures that machine telemetry and quality metrics are accurately captured and analyzed in real-time.

skills.name: Production Workflow Software Optimization
skills.description: This involves configuring Manufacturing Execution Systems to track build status and material usage. You will optimize these digital workflows to minimize downtime and maximize the utilization of your additive manufacturing fleet.

skills.name: Post-Processing Automation Systems
skills.description: Automating the cleaning, support removal, and surface finishing stages is vital for scaling AM operations. You will design and implement automated solutions that reduce manual labor and improve part quality consistency.

roles.description: As an AM Automation Engineer, you bridge the gap between additive hardware and high-volume production efficiency by architecting seamless, automated workflows. You are responsible for integrating robotic part-handling systems, post-processing cells, and the digital thread that ensures data continuity from design to final inspection. Your daily routine involves programming PLCs, troubleshooting communication protocols between 3D printers and factory management software, and optimizing cycle times to increase throughput. You operate at the intersection of mechanical engineering and software systems, acting as the technical linchpin for manufacturing operations. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous, analytical mindset, a passion for solving complex integration challenges, and the ability to maintain composure when navigating the unique, unpredictable demands of rapid additive production cycles.

roles.title: Powder Metallurgy Technician
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in Materials Science, Mechanical Engineering Technology, or a related manufacturing field is the standard requirement for this role. Many employers value coursework focused on metallurgy, chemistry, or industrial laboratory operations as a strong foundation for technical success. While an Associate's degree is the typical entry point, candidates with professional certifications in powder handling or additive manufacturing safety may be viewed as highly competitive. Some organizations offer flexibility for individuals with extensive hands-on experience in industrial powder processing or chemical handling, even if their formal education is in a different technical discipline.

skills.name: Metal Powder Characterization
skills.description: You perform essential testing to determine the physical properties of metal powders, including flowability and morphology. This ensures that the material meets the strict requirements needed for consistent layer deposition during printing.

skills.name: Inert Material Handling Protocols
skills.description: You safely manage reactive metal powders by utilizing inert gas environments or specialized glove boxes. This skill is critical to preventing oxidation and maintaining the chemical purity of sensitive industrial materials.

skills.name: Particle Size Distribution Analysis
skills.description: You utilize laser diffraction or sieve analysis to measure the particle size distribution of feedstock. Precise monitoring of these metrics is vital for achieving the correct packing density in the powder bed.

skills.name: Powder Recycling And Reclamation
skills.description: You execute systematic sieving and blending processes to reclaim used powder for future builds. Properly managing the recycling loop is essential for reducing material waste and controlling overall production costs.

skills.name: Laboratory Quality Management Systems
skills.description: You maintain detailed documentation and follow standardized operating procedures to ensure full traceability of all materials. Adhering to these quality standards is necessary to satisfy strict aerospace or medical industry regulations.

skills.name: Mechanical Equipment Maintenance
skills.description: You perform routine upkeep on sieving stations, blenders, and laboratory analytical equipment. Keeping this machinery in peak condition is necessary to prevent cross-contamination and ensure the reliability of your material testing.

roles.description: As a Powder Metallurgy Technician, you are the gatekeeper of quality for the raw materials that fuel additive manufacturing processes. Your daily responsibilities involve meticulous management of metal powder feedstock, where you execute rigorous characterization, sieving, and recycling protocols to ensure material consistency. You play a critical role in the production value chain by maintaining the integrity of powders used in laser powder bed fusion systems, directly impacting the mechanical properties of printed parts. You thrive in this role if you possess a high attention to detail, a methodical approach to laboratory safety, and a genuine curiosity for material science. By collaborating with engineers, you ensure that every batch of material meets stringent specifications for successful, high-performance industrial printing.

roles.title: AM Build Preparation Specialist
roles.degree_detail: A formal degree is sometimes required, though many employers value a mix of academic training and hands-on technical experience. An associate degree or bachelor’s degree in mechanical engineering, manufacturing technology, or industrial design is highly preferred to build a foundational understanding of additive processes. Flexibility exists for candidates with relevant vocational certifications in CAD or specific additive manufacturing software suites. Pursuing specialized training in powder bed fusion or resin-based technologies can significantly enhance your eligibility for entry-level positions.

skills.name: Advanced Build File Slicing
skills.description: You will utilize industry-standard slicing software to convert 3D models into machine-readable layers. This involves configuring critical print parameters like slice thickness, laser power, and scan strategies to ensure high-quality, repeatable outputs.

skills.name: Complex Support Structure Generation
skills.description: This skill requires you to design and place support structures that stabilize parts during the printing process. You must balance the need for thermal dissipation and structural support against the ease of manual removal.

skills.name: Efficient Part Nesting Strategies
skills.description: You are responsible for arranging parts within the build volume to maximize material utilization and throughput. Effective nesting minimizes build time and reduces the risk of collisions or thermal inconsistencies during production.

skills.name: Technical CAD Model Optimization
skills.description: You must be capable of importing and repairing CAD files to ensure they are manifold and print-ready. This often involves cleaning up geometry errors and simplifying complex meshes for seamless software interpretation.

skills.name: Material Property Understanding
skills.description: You will apply knowledge of how different materials, such as polymers or metal powders, react to heat and stress. This understanding is vital for selecting appropriate orientation and support configurations for specific prints.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: Accurate record-keeping of build logs, file revisions, and machine performance data is essential. You will track these metrics to help the team identify trends and improve overall production efficiency over time.

roles.description: As an AM Build Preparation Specialist, you serve as the critical bridge between digital CAD designs and physical production. Your day-to-day involves importing complex geometry into slicing software to orient parts for optimal surface finish, structural integrity, and build speed. You are responsible for generating precise support structures that prevent deformation and nesting parts efficiently to maximize the yield of every print run. Working closely with production engineers, you ensure that machine parameters align with material requirements to minimize failure rates. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a strong grasp of 3D geometry, and the patience to troubleshoot complex digital files before they ever reach the printer bed.

roles.title: AM Maintenance Technician
roles.degree_detail: An Associate’s Degree in Mechatronics, Industrial Maintenance, or Electrical Engineering Technology is typically required to build the necessary foundational knowledge for this role. While this degree is the standard baseline, candidates often supplement their education with technical certifications in laser safety or specific OEM machine training. Advanced study in robotics or control systems can also provide a competitive edge in the job market. Employers are sometimes flexible regarding the specific major if you can demonstrate extensive hands-on experience with complex industrial machinery and electromechanical systems.

skills.name: Industrial Electromechanical Systems Troubleshooting
skills.description: You will diagnose and resolve complex hardware issues involving sensors, motors, and controllers. This skill is essential for minimizing machine downtime and ensuring that the additive process remains stable and consistent.

skills.name: Precision Laser Optics Maintenance
skills.description: This involves the delicate alignment and cleaning of laser delivery systems and optical components. Maintaining these high-precision parts is critical for ensuring part accuracy and preventing failures during long build cycles.

skills.name: Preventive Maintenance Schedule Execution
skills.description: You will manage and perform routine service intervals to extend the lifespan of expensive additive equipment. This proactive approach prevents unexpected breakdowns and ensures the fleet remains compliant with safety standards.

skills.name: Pneumatic And Gas System Management
skills.description: You must monitor and maintain the inert gas flow and pressure systems that protect the build chamber. Proper management of these systems is vital for material integrity and overall operator safety.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Spare Parts
skills.description: You are responsible for maintaining accurate service logs and managing inventory for critical spare parts. This ensures that the maintenance team is always prepared to respond quickly to urgent equipment repairs.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Software Diagnostics
skills.description: You will interact with proprietary machine control software to interpret error codes and system logs. Proficiency here allows you to identify software-related bottlenecks that impact hardware performance and print success rates.

roles.description: As an Additive Manufacturing Maintenance Technician, you serve as the critical heartbeat of the production floor, ensuring high-precision 3D printing systems operate at peak performance. Your daily responsibilities involve diagnosing complex electromechanical failures, calibrating sensitive laser optics, and performing rigorous preventive maintenance on industrial-scale additive platforms. You act as a vital link in the manufacturing value chain, minimizing machine downtime to ensure production schedules are met with uncompromising quality standards. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, a deep passion for troubleshooting intricate hardware, and the ability to remain composed while resolving urgent technical bottlenecks. You are a collaborative problem-solver who takes immense pride in maintaining the sophisticated equipment that drives modern additive innovation.

roles.title: CNC/Finishing Specialist (AM)
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in Mechanical Engineering Technology, Manufacturing Technology, or a closely related industrial field is typically required for this role. While this formal education establishes a strong foundation in machining principles and blueprint reading, many employers are willing to consider candidates with significant vocational training or relevant industry certifications, such as NIMS credentials. Pursuing additional coursework in CAD/CAM software or advanced materials science can further enhance your employability and technical versatility. This educational path ensures you possess the essential mathematical and analytical skills required to operate advanced post-processing equipment effectively.

skills.name: Precision CNC Machining Operations
skills.description: You will utilize multi-axis CNC equipment to refine 3D-printed parts, ensuring that critical features meet strict geometric dimensioning and tolerancing standards. Proficiency in tool path optimization is essential for high-quality results.

skills.name: Advanced Surface Finishing Techniques
skills.description: This involves applying manual and automated abrasive processes to achieve specific surface roughness requirements. You must understand how different media affect various printed materials and part geometries.

skills.name: Technical Drawing And Tolerancing
skills.description: You must be capable of interpreting complex engineering blueprints and GD&T specifications. This skill ensures that your finishing work aligns perfectly with the final design intent for the end-use application.

skills.name: Thermal Treatment And Material Science
skills.description: You will manage heat treatment cycles to optimize the metallurgical properties of printed parts. Understanding material behavior is crucial for preventing distortion and ensuring required mechanical performance after processing.

skills.name: Quality Inspection And Metrology
skills.description: You will utilize precision measurement tools like calipers, micrometers, and CMMs to verify part dimensions. This ensures that every component passes final quality gates before moving to the customer.

skills.name: Secondary Manufacturing Operations
skills.description: This includes tasks like drilling, tapping, and assembly preparation on printed parts. Mastering these operations allows you to deliver fully functional, ready-to-install components to the client.

roles.description: As a CNC/Finishing Specialist in additive manufacturing, you serve as the critical bridge between raw 3D-printed builds and high-precision, market-ready components. Your day-to-day involves operating multi-axis CNC machines, managing abrasive blasting, and performing precise surface finishing to meet rigorous engineering tolerances. You are responsible for interpreting complex technical drawings, managing heat treatment cycles, and executing secondary operations that ensure structural integrity and aesthetic compliance. You play a pivotal role in the production value chain, transforming near-net-shape parts into finished assemblies. This role is best suited for a detail-oriented technician who thrives in a fast-paced environment, maintains a sharp focus on quality control, and possesses the mechanical aptitude to troubleshoot complex geometry issues with precision and consistency.

roles.title: Metrology Engineer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Manufacturing Engineering, or Materials Science is typically required for this role. While a four-year degree provides the necessary foundational knowledge in physics and engineering principles, candidates with specialized coursework in quality control or metrology are highly preferred. Pursuing professional certifications such as the ASQ Certified Calibration Technician or GD&T Professional certification can significantly strengthen your candidacy. While the educational requirement is generally firm, equivalent professional experience in a high-precision manufacturing environment may occasionally be considered in lieu of a formal degree.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning And Tolerancing
skills.description: You must apply ASME Y14.5 standards to interpret complex part drawings and define inspection requirements. This ensures that additively manufactured components meet precise functional fit and assembly specifications.

skills.name: Advanced CMM Programming And Operation
skills.description: You will develop and execute automated coordinate measuring machine programs to verify dimensional accuracy. This involves selecting appropriate probes and strategies for complex, organic AM geometries.

skills.name: 3D Scanning And Point Cloud Analysis
skills.description: Utilize structured light or laser scanning hardware to capture full-field surface data. You then compare this data against CAD models to identify deviations, porosity, or thermal distortion in printed parts.

skills.name: Measurement Systems Analysis And Calibration
skills.description: Perform Gage R&R studies to ensure your measurement processes are repeatable and reproducible. You are responsible for maintaining the integrity of all metrology equipment within the quality management system.

skills.name: Inspection Planning And Process Validation
skills.description: Design systematic inspection workflows that document quality compliance throughout the additive production cycle. This includes defining critical-to-quality features and establishing sampling plans for production batches.

skills.name: Statistical Process Control And Reporting
skills.description: Analyze measurement data to monitor process stability and identify trends in additive build quality. You translate raw inspection findings into actionable technical reports for cross-functional engineering teams.

roles.description: As a Metrology Engineer in additive manufacturing, you serve as the critical gatekeeper between digital design and physical part integrity. You are responsible for developing comprehensive inspection plans, executing precision measurements using CMMs and 3D laser scanners, and performing rigorous data analysis to validate that complex, additively manufactured geometries meet stringent tolerance requirements. You bridge the gap between design engineering and post-processing teams, ensuring that the inherent variability of AM processes is captured, documented, and mitigated. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a deep understanding of GD&T, and the ability to troubleshoot measurement system anomalies. Your technical expertise directly ensures that high-performance, mission-critical components achieve the necessary quality benchmarks for aerospace, medical, or industrial application.

roles.title: AM Product Manager
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Materials Science, or Business Administration is typically required for this senior-level position. While an undergraduate degree provides the necessary foundation, candidates often supplement their technical background with an MBA to bridge the gap between additive manufacturing innovation and corporate financial strategy. Professional certifications such as Product Management Professional (PMP) or specialized additive manufacturing credentials can further strengthen your candidacy. While formal education is a standard requirement, extensive field experience and a proven track record of bringing industrial hardware to market can sometimes provide flexibility regarding specific major requirements.

skills.name: Strategic Product Roadmap Development
skills.description: You define the long-term trajectory of additive platforms by prioritizing feature releases and material compatibility updates. This requires balancing immediate customer pain points with the overarching technical vision of the organization.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Leadership And Alignment
skills.description: You must effectively lead diverse teams, including software developers, mechanical engineers, and sales professionals. You ensure that all departments remain synchronized on project timelines and technical milestones throughout the development cycle.

skills.name: Industrial Market Analysis
skills.description: You evaluate competitive landscapes and emerging additive technology trends to identify profitable market segments. This skill involves quantifying potential ROI for clients to justify the transition from traditional manufacturing to 3D printing.

skills.name: Customer Discovery And Validation
skills.description: You engage directly with industrial partners to uncover specific application requirements and performance constraints. This involves translating complex user needs into technical specifications that the R&D team can effectively execute.

skills.name: Technical Lifecycle Management
skills.description: You manage the product through its entire lifecycle, from initial concept and prototyping to full-scale industrial production. You are responsible for identifying when to iterate on existing hardware or pivot to new platforms.

skills.name: Business Case Financial Modeling
skills.description: You build robust financial models to forecast revenue, pricing strategies, and cost-to-print metrics. This ensures that every product decision supports the company's fiscal health and competitive positioning in the additive market.

roles.description: As a Senior AM Product Manager, you own the end-to-end lifecycle of complex additive manufacturing platforms, translating intricate technical capabilities into scalable market solutions. You act as the central pivot point between R&D, application engineering, and commercial stakeholders, ensuring that hardware, software, and material development align with strategic business objectives. Your day-to-day involves synthesizing customer feedback into actionable product roadmaps, conducting rigorous market analysis to identify high-value industrial use cases, and steering cross-functional teams toward successful product launches. You thrive in this role if you possess a blend of technical fluency in 3D printing workflows and a commercial mindset, enabling you to navigate the complexities of industrial adoption while maintaining a clear vision for long-term product profitability.

roles.title: AM Training & Applications Specialist
roles.degree_detail: A bachelor’s degree in mechanical engineering, materials science, or industrial design is often preferred but not strictly required for this role. Candidates with significant hands-on experience in additive manufacturing workflows or technical instruction may substitute formal education for relevant industry certifications. Pursuing specialized credentials in specific 3D printing technologies or software platforms can further enhance your employability. Ultimately, a proven track record of technical proficiency and the ability to communicate complex concepts often carry as much weight as a traditional academic degree.

skills.name: Technical Additive Manufacturing Instruction
skills.description: You must possess the ability to teach complex 3D printing workflows to diverse audiences. This involves simplifying hardware operation, post-processing techniques, and safety protocols for operators with varying levels of technical expertise.

skills.name: Application Development And Prototyping
skills.description: You will identify and validate new use cases for additive technologies. This requires analyzing customer requirements to determine if 3D printing offers a superior cost-to-performance ratio over traditional subtractive manufacturing methods.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Content Creation
skills.description: You are responsible for writing clear, professional guides and manuals for AM systems. This ensures that users can replicate successful print results while maintaining equipment according to strict manufacturer specifications.

skills.name: Design For Additive Manufacturing Proficiency
skills.description: Mastering DFAM principles is essential for guiding customers on part consolidation, topology optimization, and lattice structures. You help users design parts that maximize the unique geometric freedoms offered by additive processes.

skills.name: Customer Facing Technical Demonstration
skills.description: You will lead live demonstrations of AM hardware and software to prospective clients. This requires confidence in presenting technical data and troubleshooting issues in real-time to maintain a high professional standard.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Software Integration
skills.description: You must be proficient in industry-standard slicing software, build preparation tools, and CAD programs. Understanding how these tools interact with the machine firmware is critical for optimizing print parameters and success.

roles.description: As an AM Training & Applications Specialist, you serve as the critical bridge between complex additive manufacturing technology and the end-user. Your day-to-day involves conducting high-impact technical training sessions, creating comprehensive documentation for hardware and software workflows, and developing innovative application case studies that showcase the unique benefits of 3D printing. You work closely with sales and engineering teams to provide live demonstrations that prove the value of AM solutions to prospective clients. You thrive in this role if you are a natural educator who possesses a deep technical curiosity, strong communication skills, and the ability to translate intricate design-for-additive-manufacturing concepts into actionable insights that empower customers to optimize their production processes effectively.

roles.title: AM Drafter
roles.degree_detail: To launch your career as an AM Drafter, you typically need an Associate’s Degree in Mechanical Drafting, Computer-Aided Design, or a closely related engineering technology field. While this degree provides the foundational knowledge of technical drawing standards, pursuing industry-recognized certifications in software like SolidWorks or AutoCAD can significantly enhance your professional profile. Some employers may consider candidates with a high school diploma if they possess extensive vocational training or a robust portfolio of technical projects. Continuing education through specialized additive manufacturing workshops often helps entry-level professionals stay current with rapid shifts in printing hardware and materials.

skills.name: Computer Aided Design Proficiency
skills.description: You will utilize industry-standard software like SolidWorks or AutoCAD to create and modify precise geometry. Mastery of these tools is essential for developing the accurate models required for additive production.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning And Tolerancing
skills.description: Applying GD&T standards ensures that your technical drawings clearly communicate critical part dimensions and constraints. This skill is vital for maintaining high quality and consistency throughout the manufacturing process.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing File Preparation
skills.description: You must understand how to convert native CAD files into formats like STL or 3MF while optimizing orientation and support structures. This process is fundamental to achieving successful, defect-free 3D prints.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Standards
skills.description: Maintaining organized records and engineering change orders is a core responsibility. You will ensure that all technical drawings adhere to strict internal and industry-wide documentation protocols for traceability and quality control.

skills.name: Design For Additive Manufacturing Principles
skills.description: Understanding the unique constraints and capabilities of 3D printing, such as overhangs and wall thickness, allows you to create buildable designs. This knowledge prevents common errors during the initial drafting phase.

skills.name: Mechanical Blueprint Interpretation
skills.description: You must be able to read and interpret complex engineering schematics and assembly drawings accurately. This foundational skill enables you to translate high-level design concepts into actionable, manufacturable technical specifications.

roles.description: As an AM Drafter, you serve as the critical bridge between conceptual engineering designs and the physical reality of the print bed. You will spend your day translating complex engineering specifications into precise 2D technical drawings and optimized 3D build files tailored for additive manufacturing processes. Your responsibilities include maintaining rigorous documentation standards, applying Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing to ensure part accuracy, and preparing files for successful production runs. You collaborate closely with manufacturing engineers to identify potential build failures before they occur, ensuring efficiency across the design-to-print workflow. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a strong aptitude for spatial reasoning, and a passion for the evolving precision of industrial 3D printing technology.

roles.title: CAM Programmer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in Mechanical Engineering Technology, Computer-Aided Drafting, or Manufacturing Technology is typically required to build the foundational knowledge for this role. Many employers value focused coursework in CNC machining and additive processes, which provide the technical literacy needed to navigate complex software environments. While this degree is the standard entry point, earning professional certifications in specific CAM software packages or additive manufacturing fundamentals can significantly enhance your candidacy. Some organizations may accept equivalent vocational training or apprenticeship experience if it demonstrates a high level of proficiency in technical drafting and machine code logic.

skills.name: CAM Software Proficiency
skills.description: You will utilize industry-standard platforms like Mastercam or HSMWorks to translate 3D models into functional machine instructions. Mastering these tools is essential for optimizing toolpaths and ensuring successful part production.

skills.name: G-Code And Machine Logic
skills.description: Understanding G-code is critical for troubleshooting machine behavior and manual code adjustments. You must be able to interpret and write code to ensure seamless communication between your digital files and hardware.

skills.name: Additive Build Preparation
skills.description: This involves orienting parts within the build volume and generating support structures to prevent warping or print failure. Proper preparation ensures that the geometry remains stable throughout the entire additive process.

skills.name: CNC Machining Fundamentals
skills.description: Since many additive parts require secondary finishing, you need a solid grasp of CNC subtractive workflows. Knowing how to program for post-printing operations ensures that parts meet final dimensional specifications.

skills.name: Technical Blueprint Reading
skills.description: You must accurately interpret complex engineering drawings and GD&T specifications to ensure the final product meets design intent. This skill is vital for verifying that your toolpaths align with critical tolerances.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning And Tolerancing
skills.description: Applying GD&T standards allows you to effectively communicate and control part features during the programming phase. This ensures that the manufactured components are consistent, interchangeable, and meet high-quality engineering standards.

roles.description: As an entry-level CAM Programmer in additive manufacturing, you bridge the gap between digital design and physical production by translating complex CAD models into optimized machine code. Your day-to-day involves nesting parts, generating precise toolpaths for both additive build processes and subtractive finishing operations, and verifying printability through simulation software. You are responsible for ensuring that build files meet strict tolerance requirements while minimizing material waste and cycle times. Working closely with design engineers and machine operators, you play a vital role in the production value chain by maintaining technical accuracy throughout the workflow. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a strong aptitude for spatial reasoning, and a passion for solving intricate manufacturing challenges.

roles.title: Reverse Engineering Technician (AM)
roles.degree_detail: To enter this field, you typically need an Associate's Degree in Mechanical Engineering Technology, Manufacturing Engineering, or a closely related industrial design discipline. While an associate’s degree is the standard entry requirement, candidates with vocational certifications in metrology or CAD software often stand out to hiring managers. Advanced study or specialized training in 3D scanning hardware and inspection software can provide a significant competitive advantage. Employers are sometimes flexible if you can demonstrate equivalent technical proficiency through a strong portfolio of 3D modeling projects or relevant hands-on manufacturing experience.

skills.name: CAD Reconstruction And Modeling
skills.description: You will translate complex point cloud data into parametric 3D models. This requires proficiency in surfacing tools to ensure the final digital geometry accurately represents the physical legacy part.

skills.name: 3D Scanning And Data Capture
skills.description: You must operate high-resolution 3D scanners to capture precise geometric data from physical objects. This involves managing scan alignment, resolution settings, and environmental factors to ensure high-fidelity digital representations.

skills.name: Point Cloud Processing Software
skills.description: Using software like Geomagic or Polyworks, you will clean, register, and merge raw scan data. This process is essential for removing noise and preparing a watertight mesh for downstream engineering.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning And Tolerancing
skills.description: Applying GD&T standards ensures that your reconstructed models meet strict engineering requirements. You will use these principles to verify that replacement parts function correctly within their intended mechanical assemblies.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Design Principles
skills.description: You must understand how to optimize digital models for specific 3D printing technologies. This includes accounting for support structures, build orientation, and material shrinkage to ensure successful, accurate print results.

skills.name: Metrology And Part Inspection
skills.description: You will perform comparative analysis between physical parts and CAD models to validate accuracy. This skill ensures that all produced components adhere to required dimensional standards before final manufacturing.

roles.description: As a Reverse Engineering Technician in additive manufacturing, you serve as the critical bridge between legacy physical components and modern digital production. Your day-to-day involves operating precision 3D scanners to capture complex part geometries, transforming raw point cloud data into accurate, printable CAD models. You are responsible for ensuring that reconstructed designs meet original specifications while optimizing them for additive manufacturing constraints. You work closely with design engineers and production teams to validate dimensions and tolerances, ensuring seamless reproduction of out-of-production or replacement parts. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a passion for solving geometric puzzles, and the patience required to refine digital meshes into high-quality, manufacturable engineering files.

roles.title: Generative Design Engineer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Aerospace Engineering, or a closely related computational design field is required for this position. Candidates with a strong foundation in CAD modeling and finite element analysis often transition most effectively into these specialized workflows. While a bachelor's degree is the standard entry point, pursuing advanced certifications in specific generative software platforms or a Master's degree in Additive Manufacturing can significantly enhance your technical proficiency. The field is increasingly interdisciplinary, so familiarity with materials science or computer-aided engineering remains highly advantageous for long-term career growth.

skills.name: Computational Generative Design Workflows
skills.description: You utilize advanced software to define design constraints and objectives, allowing algorithms to explore thousands of iterations to find the most efficient geometry for additive production.

skills.name: Topology Optimization And Simulation
skills.description: This skill involves applying mathematical models to remove unnecessary material from a part while maintaining structural integrity, ensuring that components are perfectly optimized for their specific load-bearing requirements.

skills.name: Complex Lattice Structure Development
skills.description: You design and integrate intricate, non-uniform lattice structures within parts to manage energy absorption, thermal conductivity, or weight reduction, which are critical advantages of the additive manufacturing process.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Design Constraints
skills.description: You possess a deep understanding of Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM) principles, ensuring that your generative outputs account for support requirements, build orientation, and thermal distortion during the printing process.

skills.name: Advanced CAD Software Proficiency
skills.description: You demonstrate high-level expertise in industry-standard tools like nTopology or Fusion 360, utilizing these platforms to manage complex geometry datasets and automate repetitive design tasks effectively.

skills.name: Finite Element Analysis Integration
skills.description: You perform structural validation of generative designs by running simulation studies to confirm that optimized parts meet all safety, fatigue, and performance targets before they proceed to the print queue.

roles.description: As a Generative Design Engineer, you leverage algorithmic workflows to push the boundaries of additive manufacturing, transforming complex structural requirements into highly optimized, lightweight geometries. Your day-to-day involves defining design spaces, setting performance constraints, and iterating through topology optimization to produce parts that are impossible to manufacture via traditional methods. You act as the critical bridge between conceptual engineering and production, ensuring that internal lattices, conformal cooling channels, and organic structures are manufacturable and print-ready. You thrive in this role if you possess a rigorous analytical mindset, a passion for computational geometry, and the technical patience to refine complex design iterations. By collaborating closely with AM technicians and simulation experts, you drive innovation that maximizes material efficiency and functional performance.

roles.title: Tooling & Fixture Engineer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Manufacturing Engineering, or a closely related field is typically required to build the necessary technical foundation for this role. While a four-year degree is the standard, candidates with significant portfolio experience in CAD-based tooling design may occasionally be considered. Pursuing professional certifications in specific additive manufacturing software or GD&T standards can significantly enhance your candidacy. Continuing education through specialized industry workshops or a Master’s degree focusing on advanced materials and additive processes is often encouraged for those seeking to specialize further.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Tooling Design
skills.description: You apply design-for-additive-manufacturing principles to create custom jigs and fixtures. This involves balancing printability with mechanical performance to ensure tools are durable, lightweight, and perfectly suited for the build environment.

skills.name: Geometric Dimensioning And Tolerancing
skills.description: You utilize GD&T to define precise specifications for complex 3D-printed components. This ensures that your tooling designs maintain strict assembly requirements and interface correctly with conventional manufacturing equipment on the floor.

skills.name: Advanced CAD Modeling Proficiency
skills.description: You leverage software like SolidWorks to develop detailed 3D models and technical drawings. Mastery of parametric modeling is essential for rapidly iterating on fixture designs based on real-time production feedback.

skills.name: Manufacturing Process Integration
skills.description: You analyze production workflows to identify bottlenecks where custom tooling can drive efficiency. This requires a deep understanding of both additive and subtractive manufacturing to select the best production method.

skills.name: Mechanical Engineering Fundamentals
skills.description: You apply core engineering principles like stress analysis and material selection to your designs. Ensuring your fixtures can withstand the physical rigors of the shop floor is critical for reliable performance.

skills.name: Technical Validation And Testing
skills.description: You execute hands-on testing and verification of new tooling designs before full-scale implementation. This process involves gathering data on tool longevity and performance to iteratively improve your mechanical solutions.

roles.description: As a Tooling & Fixture Engineer, you bridge the gap between additive manufacturing capabilities and high-volume production efficiency. You spend your day designing custom jigs, fixtures, and end-of-arm tooling that optimize print orientation, minimize support structures, and streamline downstream assembly workflows. By collaborating closely with machine operators and design teams, you ensure that 3D-printed components meet rigorous dimensional tolerances while reducing overall lead times. You are responsible for the full lifecycle of tooling, from initial CAD conceptualization to final validation on the production floor. The ideal candidate thrives in a fast-paced environment, possesses a sharp eye for geometric precision, and enjoys solving complex mechanical challenges that push the boundaries of what additive manufacturing can achieve in industrial settings.

roles.title: AM Solutions Architect
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical, Aerospace, or Materials Engineering is the standard academic foundation for this senior role. While an undergraduate degree is required, many professionals in this capacity possess a Master’s degree or PhD in Additive Manufacturing or Systems Engineering to deepen their technical specialization. Pursuing professional certifications such as the SME Additive Manufacturing Certification or Six Sigma Black Belt can significantly enhance your candidacy. Flexibility exists for candidates with extensive industry experience who can demonstrate equivalent technical mastery and a proven track record of architecting complex manufacturing systems.

skills.name: Enterprise Additive Manufacturing Strategy
skills.description: You develop and execute high-level roadmaps that align additive capabilities with corporate business goals. This involves evaluating cost-benefit analyses and scalability to ensure long-term manufacturing success across the organization.

skills.name: Multi-Process Systems Architecture
skills.description: You design integrated production environments that utilize diverse printing technologies and post-processing workflows. This skill ensures that disparate hardware and software components function as a cohesive, reliable manufacturing ecosystem.

skills.name: Advanced Design For Additive Manufacturing
skills.description: You apply sophisticated DfAM principles to optimize parts for weight reduction, performance, and printability. You lead efforts to rethink traditional geometries to fully leverage the freedom provided by modern additive processes.

skills.name: Production Qualification And Validation
skills.description: You establish rigorous protocols for material and process validation to meet strict industry standards. This ensures that every additive component produced consistently adheres to safety, quality, and regulatory requirements.

skills.name: Technical Stakeholder Project Management
skills.description: You communicate complex technical architectural concepts to non-technical stakeholders and cross-functional teams. This requires managing expectations while driving alignment on project scope, timelines, and critical performance milestones.

skills.name: Material Selection And Characterization
skills.description: You evaluate and select advanced polymers, metals, or composites based on specific mechanical and environmental performance needs. Your deep understanding of material properties is essential for successful part qualification.

roles.description: As an AM Solutions Architect, you serve as the strategic bridge between complex enterprise requirements and high-performance additive manufacturing production ecosystems. You lead the end-to-end architectural design, meticulously selecting optimal materials, printing technologies, and post-processing workflows to ensure seamless integration into industrial value chains. Your day-to-day involves navigating technical trade-offs in DfAM, overseeing rigorous qualification protocols, and aligning production planning with overarching business objectives. You thrive in this high-impact role if you possess a systems-thinking mindset, an ability to translate abstract customer needs into scalable technical roadmaps, and the technical maturity to navigate cross-functional stakeholder environments. You are the architect of reliability, ensuring that every additive solution is not only innovative but also economically viable and structurally robust for mission-critical applications.

roles.title: Lead Generative Design Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Aerospace Engineering, or Materials Science is the standard entry requirement for this senior-level position. While a four-year degree provides the foundational knowledge, many professionals in this field pursue a Master’s degree or specialized certifications in computational design to gain a competitive edge. Relevant coursework in finite element analysis and solid mechanics is highly valued, though significant practical experience in additive manufacturing environments can often supplement formal advanced academic credentials.

skills.name: Advanced Topology Optimization Techniques
skills.description: You will utilize complex algorithms to distribute material within a given design space to meet performance targets. This skill is critical for achieving maximum strength-to-weight ratios in high-performance additive parts.

skills.name: Multi-Physics Simulation And Analysis
skills.description: This involves running coupled simulations to predict how parts behave under thermal, structural, and fluid dynamic loads. Proficiency here ensures your designs meet rigorous industry requirements before physical prototyping begins.

skills.name: Complex Lattice Structure Design
skills.description: You will engineer intricate, non-uniform lattice architectures to control energy absorption and heat transfer. Mastering these geometries is essential for leveraging the full geometric freedom offered by additive manufacturing.

skills.name: Technical Leadership And Mentorship
skills.description: As a lead, you are responsible for guiding design workflows and fostering growth within your engineering team. You must effectively communicate complex design strategies to stakeholders and junior staff members.

skills.name: Computational Design Software Proficiency
skills.description: You must demonstrate expert-level command of platforms like nTopology or similar generative design tools. This expertise allows you to automate complex design iterations and maintain high-quality engineering standards.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Process Knowledge
skills.description: Deep understanding of various print technologies is required to align design intent with specific machine capabilities. You must account for build orientation, support structures, and thermal distortion during the design phase.

roles.description: As a Lead Generative Design Engineer, you serve as the technical authority for complex additive manufacturing projects, driving innovation through advanced computational workflows. You spend your days architecting sophisticated generative strategies, overseeing the implementation of design-for-additive-manufacturing standards, and mentoring junior engineers to elevate the team’s collective technical proficiency. You sit at the critical intersection of product development and manufacturing, translating high-level performance requirements into optimized, lightweight, and high-performance geometries. You thrive in this role if you possess a rigorous analytical mindset, a passion for solving complex structural challenges, and the ability to bridge the gap between abstract mathematical optimization and physical production constraints, ensuring every design is both theoretically perfect and reliably manufacturable at scale.

roles.title: AM Standards & Compliance Lead
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical, Materials, or Aerospace Engineering is the standard requirement for this role. While a four-year degree provides the necessary technical foundation, many professionals further distinguish themselves by pursuing advanced degrees or specialized certifications in Quality Engineering or Regulatory Affairs. Professional certifications from organizations like ASQ or specific additive manufacturing credentials are highly preferred and often viewed as essential for navigating complex certification audits. While the degree requirement is firm, candidates with significant experience in high-compliance sectors like aerospace or medical devices are often prioritized over those with purely academic backgrounds.

skills.name: Regulatory Standards Interpretation
skills.description: You must possess an expert-level ability to interpret and apply ASTM F42 and ISO/ASTM 52900 standards. This involves translating complex regulatory requirements into practical, internal engineering specifications for additive manufacturing workflows.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Qualification Frameworks
skills.description: You are responsible for designing and executing comprehensive qualification and certification programs. This includes establishing rigorous testing protocols to validate material properties and geometric accuracy for critical-use AM components.

skills.name: Quality Management System Oversight
skills.description: You lead the maintenance of quality management systems specifically tailored for additive production environments. This ensures consistent documentation, process repeatability, and traceability throughout the entire component lifecycle from design to final inspection.

skills.name: Technical Liaison And Advocacy
skills.description: You actively represent the organization within global standards committees such as ISO TC 261. This requires strong communication skills to advocate for internal interests while staying ahead of emerging industry regulations.

skills.name: Risk Assessment And Mitigation
skills.description: You perform systematic risk assessments to identify potential failure modes in additive processes. Your role is to implement robust mitigation strategies that satisfy FAA, FDA, or other relevant regulatory oversight requirements.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Compliance Leadership
skills.description: You manage the alignment between design engineering, material science, and production teams. Your leadership ensures that compliance is integrated into the design phase rather than treated as a final, reactive step.

roles.description: As an AM Standards & Compliance Lead, you serve as the critical bridge between complex regulatory landscapes and high-stakes additive manufacturing production. You own the strategy for internal qualification frameworks, ensuring every printed component meets rigorous aerospace, medical, or industrial benchmarks. Your day-to-day involves active participation in ASTM F42 and ISO TC 261 committees, where you translate evolving global standards into actionable internal protocols for design validation and material characterization. You operate at the intersection of engineering and quality assurance, guiding cross-functional teams to maintain compliance while fostering innovation. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous attention to detail, a talent for interpreting technical policy, and the leadership capacity to drive organizational alignment within highly regulated manufacturing environments.

roles.title: Lead Tooling Engineer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Manufacturing Engineering, or a closely related field is typically required to build the foundational knowledge necessary for this role. While a four-year degree is the standard entry point, many successful candidates pursue a Master’s degree in Additive Manufacturing or Systems Engineering to gain a competitive edge. Professional certifications such as the SME Additive Manufacturing Certification or Six Sigma Black Belt are highly valued, as they demonstrate a commitment to rigorous quality standards and process optimization. While formal education is essential, industry-specific experience often carries equal weight, and candidates with a strong portfolio of successful production-grade tooling projects are frequently prioritized.

skills.name: Advanced Fixture Design Leadership
skills.description: You will oversee the end-to-end design process for complex, functional fixtures, ensuring they meet rigorous tolerance requirements and integrate seamlessly with additive production hardware.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Tooling Strategy
skills.description: This involves developing comprehensive roadmaps for tooling production that balance material selection, build orientation, and post-processing requirements to maximize cost-efficiency and part performance.

skills.name: Cross-Functional Engineering Team Leadership
skills.description: You will guide multidisciplinary teams by providing technical mentorship, facilitating design reviews, and fostering a culture of continuous improvement within the additive manufacturing department.

skills.name: Design For Additive Manufacturing Principles
skills.description: Applying DfAM expertise allows you to optimize tooling geometries for weight reduction, internal cooling channels, and part consolidation, ensuring superior performance compared to traditional machining methods.

skills.name: Production Tooling Lifecycle Management
skills.description: You are responsible for the entire lifecycle of tooling assets, from initial concept and prototyping to final production validation and long-term maintenance in a high-volume facility.

skills.name: Tolerance Analysis And Quality Control
skills.description: You must implement robust metrology processes and analytical techniques to verify that all additive fixtures maintain the precision required for high-accuracy industrial manufacturing applications.

roles.description: As a Lead Tooling Engineer in Additive Manufacturing, you serve as the technical authority for the development and lifecycle management of complex jigs, fixtures, and end-of-arm tooling. You bridge the gap between design intent and production execution, driving standardization across diverse product lines to optimize throughput and repeatability. Your day-to-day involves mentoring junior engineers, vetting advanced material applications, and collaborating with cross-functional teams to integrate additive processes into traditional manufacturing workflows. You thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for design-for-additive-manufacturing principles, a proactive approach to solving complex production bottlenecks, and the strategic vision to scale tooling solutions that significantly reduce lead times and improve overall manufacturing efficiency within high-stakes industrial environments.

roles.title: Powder Handling Technician (AM)
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in Materials Science, Mechanical Engineering Technology, or a related industrial field is the standard requirement for this entry-level position. While a two-year degree provides the necessary technical foundation, candidates with certifications in hazardous materials handling or additive manufacturing processes often stand out. Some employers may consider equivalent work experience in a laboratory or chemical processing environment if a formal degree is still in progress. Continued study in metallurgy or additive manufacturing technologies can further accelerate your career growth within the materials and process development cluster.

skills.name: Inert Atmosphere Powder Handling
skills.description: You will manage the transfer and storage of reactive metal powders within controlled argon-purged environments. This prevents oxidation and ensures the structural integrity of the material before it enters the printer.

skills.name: Powder Sieving And Blending
skills.description: This involves operating mechanical sieving equipment to remove oversized particles or contaminants from recycled powder. You ensure that the feedstock meets specified particle size distribution requirements for consistent machine performance.

skills.name: Contamination Control And Traceability
skills.description: You maintain strict documentation for every powder lot to ensure full material traceability. This prevents cross-contamination between different alloy types, which is essential for maintaining the metallurgical quality of printed parts.

skills.name: Industrial Safety And PPE Protocols
skills.description: You must strictly adhere to safety standards when working with reactive or fine powders. This includes the correct use of respirators, fire-resistant clothing, and specialized extraction systems to mitigate inhalation or explosion risks.

skills.name: Laboratory Quality Testing
skills.description: You perform basic diagnostic checks on powder samples to verify flowability and moisture content. These tests confirm that the material is within the operational window required for successful additive manufacturing builds.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: You will record batch data and equipment maintenance logs with high precision. Accurate reporting is vital for process validation and ensuring that all material usage complies with internal quality management systems.

roles.description: As a Powder Handling Technician, you serve as the critical gatekeeper for the quality of raw materials used in additive manufacturing production. You are responsible for the precise conditioning, sieving, and blending of metal or polymer powders, ensuring they meet the stringent particle size distribution and purity standards required for high-performance builds. Working within an inert-atmosphere environment, you manage powder lot traceability and contamination control protocols to maintain build consistency. You act as a vital link in the production chain, ensuring that machines are fed with pristine feedstock to prevent costly defects. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a strong commitment to safety, and the ability to follow rigorous technical procedures.

roles.title: Materials Test Technician (AM)
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in Materials Science, Mechanical Engineering Technology, or a closely related technical field is the standard requirement for this entry-level position. While the associate's degree provides the necessary foundation in testing methodologies and lab safety, candidates with relevant certifications in non-destructive testing or quality assurance are often given preference. This educational path is generally considered a firm requirement, though equivalent hands-on experience in a metallurgical or testing laboratory may occasionally be considered as a substitute. Pursuing further certifications in specific ASTM testing standards can significantly accelerate your professional development within the additive manufacturing sector.

skills.name: Mechanical And Tensile Testing
skills.description: You will operate universal testing machines to apply controlled forces to AM coupons. This ensures that printed parts meet the required yield strength, elongation, and ultimate tensile strength specifications.

skills.name: Metallographic Sample Preparation
skills.description: This involves cutting, mounting, grinding, and polishing printed samples to prepare them for microscopic analysis. Proper preparation is essential for identifying porosity, lack-of-fusion, or other build defects.

skills.name: Hardness Testing Methodologies
skills.description: You will perform Rockwell or Vickers hardness tests to verify the surface and core mechanical properties of metallic parts. This data confirms that the thermal history of the print was consistent.

skills.name: Laboratory Safety And Compliance
skills.description: You must strictly adhere to OSHA standards and internal safety protocols when handling hazardous materials or operating heavy testing equipment. Maintaining a clean, organized, and safe laboratory environment is mandatory.

skills.name: Data Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: You are responsible for accurately recording test results and maintaining digital logs for traceability. Clear reporting is vital for the engineering team to validate build parameters and quality standards.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Process Knowledge
skills.description: Understanding the fundamental principles of powder bed fusion or directed energy deposition helps you correlate test failures with specific print orientations or support structures used during the build.

roles.description: As a Materials Test Technician in Additive Manufacturing, you serve as the critical gatekeeper for build quality, ensuring that printed components meet rigorous structural specifications. Your daily routine involves preparing witness coupons, performing precise mechanical testing, and documenting physical property data to validate the integrity of the print process. You work closely with engineers and production teams to identify anomalies, playing a vital role in the feedback loop that optimizes machine parameters and material performance. You will thrive in this position if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a strong commitment to laboratory safety protocols, and a genuine curiosity about how microscopic material behavior translates into the performance of complex, high-stakes 3D-printed industrial components.

roles.title: Metallography Technician (AM)
roles.degree_detail: The standard entry path for this role is an Associate’s Degree in Materials Science, Metallurgical Engineering Technology, or a closely related manufacturing discipline. While a two-year degree is the primary requirement, candidates with vocational certifications in non-destructive testing or laboratory science are often highly competitive. Some employers may consider equivalent experience in a quality control environment as a substitute for formal education. Pursuing additional certifications from organizations like ASM International can further enhance your technical expertise as you begin your career.

skills.name: Metallographic Sample Preparation Techniques
skills.description: You will master the systematic process of sectioning, mounting, grinding, and polishing metal coupons. Achieving a scratch-free, mirror-like finish is vital for accurate microstructural analysis of additive components.

skills.name: Optical Microscopy And Imaging
skills.description: This skill involves the operation of advanced optical microscopes to capture high-resolution images of grain structures. You must precisely document features like pore distribution and un-melted powder particles.

skills.name: Chemical Etching For Microstructure
skills.description: You will apply specific chemical reagents to polished surfaces to reveal grain boundaries and secondary phases. This process is essential for identifying microstructural variations caused by thermal gradients during printing.

skills.name: Porosity And Defect Analysis
skills.description: You will interpret micrographs to quantify internal defects such as gas pores, lack-of-fusion voids, and cracks. This data is critical for validating that printed parts meet structural integrity standards.

skills.name: Laboratory Safety And Protocol
skills.description: You must strictly adhere to chemical handling protocols and laboratory safety standards. Proper management of hazardous etching agents and mechanical equipment is essential for maintaining a secure and compliant workspace.

skills.name: Data Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: You are responsible for organizing and archiving your imaging results into technical reports. Clear communication of your findings ensures that engineering teams can make informed decisions regarding build quality.

roles.description: As an entry-level Metallography Technician in additive manufacturing, you serve as the critical bridge between the build plate and final part certification. Your day-to-day work involves precision sectioning, mounting, and polishing of 3D-printed metal samples to reveal their internal material characteristics. You are responsible for capturing high-quality optical micrographs to evaluate porosity, grain orientation, and potential fusion defects, ensuring every part meets stringent engineering specifications. By documenting these structural findings, you provide the essential data that process engineers use to optimize print parameters and refine alloy performance. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous eye for detail, a patient approach to delicate laboratory procedures, and a genuine passion for materials science and advanced manufacturing technology.

roles.title: AM Process Lab Assistant
roles.degree_detail: An Associate's Degree in Additive Manufacturing, Mechanical Engineering Technology, or Materials Science is typically required for this entry-level role. Programs that emphasize hands-on lab experience with 3D printing hardware or CNC machinery are highly preferred by hiring managers. While the degree is a standard baseline, candidates may bolster their applications with industry-recognized certifications in specific software platforms or laser safety training. In some instances, equivalent vocational training combined with significant technical shop experience can serve as a flexible alternative to a traditional degree.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Machine Operation
skills.description: You will operate various 3D printing systems, ensuring machines are calibrated correctly before starting builds. This involves loading materials, clearing build chambers, and performing routine maintenance to ensure consistent print quality.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Data Logging
skills.description: Accurate record-keeping is vital for R&D success. You must log process parameters, build times, and environmental conditions into digital systems, ensuring that every experiment is traceable and reproducible for the engineering team.

skills.name: Sample Preparation And Metallography
skills.description: You will prepare printed parts for testing by cutting, mounting, and polishing specimens. This process is essential for evaluating material density, porosity, and microstructure through post-build analysis and microscopic inspection.

skills.name: Lab Safety And Compliance
skills.description: You are responsible for maintaining a safe workspace by following strict protocols for handling powders and chemicals. This includes using proper personal protective equipment and managing hazardous material waste according to regulations.

skills.name: Basic Troubleshooting And Diagnostics
skills.description: You will identify and resolve common machine errors during print cycles. This requires a logical approach to diagnosing mechanical or software issues to minimize downtime and prevent the loss of expensive test builds.

skills.name: Quality Control Inspection Techniques
skills.description: You will perform dimensional checks and visual inspections on printed parts to ensure they meet project specifications. Using basic metrology tools, you will identify defects early to assist in the process refinement cycle.

roles.description: As an AM Process Lab Assistant, you serve as the backbone of the R&D department, ensuring the seamless execution of additive manufacturing experiments. Your day-to-day involves meticulous sample preparation, conducting parameter sweeps, and maintaining rigorous build logs to track material performance. You are responsible for monitoring machine operations during critical R&D runs, troubleshooting minor hardware hiccups, and ensuring lab safety protocols are strictly followed. By providing high-quality data and well-documented samples, you enable engineers to refine complex printing processes more efficiently. You will thrive in this role if you are a detail-oriented problem solver who enjoys hands-on technical work, possesses a high degree of patience, and maintains a structured approach to documentation in a fast-paced, innovative laboratory environment.

roles.title: Extrusion Process Engineer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Mechanical Engineering, Materials Science, or Chemical Engineering is the standard requirement for this position. While a four-year degree provides the necessary foundation in thermodynamics and mechanics, candidates with coursework in polymer science or additive manufacturing technologies are highly preferred. Pursuing specialized certifications in CAD/CAM software or industrial robotics can further strengthen your candidacy. Although a Bachelor’s is generally sufficient, some employers may value a Master’s degree if your research focused heavily on rheology or advanced manufacturing process development.

skills.name: Polymer Extrusion Process Control
skills.description: You manage the precise thermal and mechanical variables of extrusion, ensuring consistent material deposition. This involves balancing flow rates and nozzle temperatures to maintain part quality across large-format print jobs.

skills.name: Advanced FDM FFF Tuning
skills.description: You calibrate slicer settings and hardware configurations to optimize layer adhesion and surface finish. This skill is critical for mitigating common extrusion defects like warping, stringing, or under-extrusion in complex geometries.

skills.name: Material Rheology And Characterization
skills.description: You analyze how different polymer feedstocks behave under shear and heat during the extrusion process. Understanding these flow properties is essential for selecting the right materials for specific industrial applications.

skills.name: Pellet Feedstock Processing Optimization
skills.description: You oversee the transition from traditional filament to pellet-based extrusion systems. This involves managing hopper feed rates and screw mechanics to achieve higher throughput and faster build times for large parts.

skills.name: Process Parameter Data Analysis
skills.description: You use statistical methods to evaluate print data and correlate machine settings with final part performance. This evidence-based approach allows you to iterate on processes and standardize quality control protocols.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Hardware Maintenance
skills.description: You perform hands-on diagnostics and mechanical adjustments on extrusion systems to ensure operational uptime. This includes maintaining print heads, heating elements, and motion systems to prevent unplanned downtime during critical builds.

roles.description: As an Extrusion Process Engineer, you serve as the technical bridge between material science and machine performance within the additive manufacturing lifecycle. You spend your days optimizing large-format FFF systems and pellet extrusion hardware to ensure consistent print quality and structural integrity. You are responsible for characterizing feedstock rheology, fine-tuning thermal profiles, and qualifying new materials for production-ready applications. Working closely with design and operations teams, you troubleshoot process bottlenecks and implement iterative improvements to increase build efficiency. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, a passion for hardware experimentation, and the ability to translate complex data into actionable process parameters that push the boundaries of current extrusion-based additive manufacturing capabilities.

roles.title: AM Polymer Materials Engineer
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor's Degree in Materials Science, Chemical Engineering, Polymer Science, or a closely related engineering discipline is required for this role. While a four-year degree provides the necessary foundational knowledge, many employers highly value candidates who have pursued relevant coursework in thermodynamics, rheology, or organic chemistry. Advanced study, such as a Master’s degree, can be beneficial but is not strictly mandatory if you possess strong practical experience. Professional certifications in additive manufacturing or specific software suites can further distinguish your profile, though hands-on laboratory experience is often viewed as the most critical indicator of your readiness.

skills.name: Polymer Characterization And Testing
skills.description: You will utilize techniques like DSC, TGA, and DMA to analyze the thermal and mechanical properties of feedstocks. This ensures materials meet strict performance benchmarks before being approved for production.

skills.name: Photopolymer And Thermoplastic Processing
skills.description: You must understand the chemistry of UV-curable resins and the melt-flow behavior of thermoplastics. This knowledge allows you to optimize print settings for specific polymer chemistries and hardware platforms.

skills.name: Additive Manufacturing Feedstock Qualification
skills.description: This involves establishing standardized testing protocols to validate new materials for industrial use. You will document material performance to ensure consistent quality and reliability across all printed additive components.

skills.name: Statistical Analysis And Data Modeling
skills.description: You will apply statistical methods to interpret experimental data and identify process trends. This skill is essential for performing root cause analysis and refining material parameters to reduce print variability.

skills.name: Design For Additive Manufacturing Principles
skills.description: Understanding how material properties interact with part geometry is crucial for successful prints. You will collaborate with design teams to ensure that selected materials are compatible with intended structural requirements.

skills.name: Technical Documentation And Reporting
skills.description: You will maintain comprehensive records of material test results and process modifications. Clear documentation is vital for regulatory compliance and for maintaining a robust knowledge base for future development projects.

roles.description: As an AM Polymer Materials Engineer, you serve as the technical bridge between raw chemical feedstocks and high-performance end-use components. You spend your days characterizing polymer behaviors, qualifying new resins or powders for SLA, DLP, FDM, and SLS platforms, and optimizing print parameters to ensure material consistency. You are responsible for executing rigorous testing protocols, analyzing thermal and mechanical data, and troubleshooting print failures to refine production workflows. Working closely with design and manufacturing teams, you ensure that material properties meet stringent application requirements across diverse sectors. You will thrive in this role if you possess a meticulous analytical mindset, a deep curiosity for polymer science, and the ability to translate complex material data into actionable process improvements for industrial-scale additive manufacturing.

roles.title: Metallurgical Engineer (AM)
roles.degree_detail: A Bachelor’s Degree in Metallurgical Engineering, Materials Science, or a closely related engineering discipline is required to establish your foundational knowledge. Many professionals further distinguish themselves by pursuing a Master’s degree in Materials Engineering, which provides deeper expertise in solidification theory and powder characterization.
